Speaker: Rajiv Pratap Rudy — Saran
Speech: I wish to draw the attention of the Hon'ble Minister for Power and the Hon'ble Minister for Environment, Forest and Climate Change to a serious concern affecting Delhi's air quality. As per an analysis of the Central Electricity Authority, out of 21 thermal power units within a 300 km radius of Delhi that do not have Flue Gas Desulphurisation (FGD) systems, 15 units across 6 thermal power plants are still operational without FGD, resulting in continuous sulfur dioxide (SO₂) emissions, a major cause of respiratory diseases and acid rain. This is in violation of mandatory emission norms notified by the Government. I urge the Government to place on record the compliance status of these plants, fix strict deadlines, and initiate penal action, including suspension of non-compliant units, in the interest of public health. Re: Need to include Mangalore International Airport in the list of “Points of Call” under bilateral air service agreements CAPTAIN BRIJESH CHOWTA (DAKSHINA KANNADA): I wish to raise the issue of the non-inclusion of Mangaluru International Airport (MIA) in the list of “Points of Call” under India’s bilateral Air Services Agreements. MIA, located in Dakshina Kannada district, serves as the principal aviation gateway for coastal Karnataka and adjoining parts of Kerala. The airport handled approximately 23.4 lakh passengers, including 7.15 lakh international passengers, and over 16,800 aircraft movements in 2024-25, registering over 15 per cent annual growth. The Tulu Nadu region has a large overseas workforce in the Gulf and Middle East that depends on Mangaluru for travel. The absence of PoC status forces passengers to route through Bengaluru or Kochi, increasing travel time and costs. Allowing foreign carriers to operate directly to Mangaluru will ease this burden and naturally expand travel options for the diaspora. Enhanced connectivity will also boost trade and investment, supporting industries such as marine exports, arecanut, cashew, and petrochemicals, and complementing the growth of Mangalore SEZ. Furthermore, better international access will promote tourism and educational mobility, enabling easier travel for students and visitors to institutions like NITK and MIT. I urge the Hon'ble Minister of Civil Aviation to accord PoC status to Mangaluru International Airport. Speaker: Saugata Roy — Dum Dum
Speech: I thank the Government of India for the historic efforts made to rescue the Naipparhwa Buddhist remains. These remains, found by Nivinam Claxton Peppe in 1898 at Naipparhwa in Nasirathanagar, are an invaluable heritage for millions of Buddhists and also a symbol of India's tradition of compassion, peace and non-violence. When these remains were listed for auction at ‘Sotheby’s’ in Hong Kong in May 2025, it was a challenge to India’s cultural identity. At such a moment, the government under the leadership of the Prime Minister quickly intervened and stopped the auction and set an example of Virasat Sir moment by bringing him back to India. The government further gave new impetus to Buddhist cultural diplomacy, India-Bhutan friendship and global soft power by sending these remains to the Bhutanese administration. This has further strengthened India's leadership role in these unprecedented times. Finally, I request that these sacred relics should be relocated to the Kanpivastu Sangrahalaya located in Nasithanagar, so that the local economy, tourism and Buddhist circuit can get a boost and Nasithanagar can be placed more prominently on the world Buddhist map. (Inat) Re: Construction of Modern Sports Complex in Kushi Nagar Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Hanuman Beniwal — Nagaur
Speech: I want to draw your attention towards Kushinagar Lok Sabha constituency. The possibility of sports activities in Kushinagar Lok Sabha constituency is very high because the youth here are very enthusiastic towards sports. People here are interested in cricket, football, kabaddi, and other traditional sports. Promoting agricultural activities in Kushinagar will not only lead to physical and mental development of the youth but will also develop agricultural culture in the area. There is no big stadium or sports complex for sports and youth development in this area, due to which the youth here are not getting opportunities to participate in sports activities. Therefore, I urge you to take necessary action to build a modern sports complex in Kushinagar Lok Sabha constituency. This will provide opportunities to the youth here to participate in sports activities and develop their skills. (Inat) Re: Need for doubling of the Indore-Gwalior Rail route in Madhya Pradesh

Speaker: Rodmal Nagar — Rajgarh
Speech: The much awaited electrification work of the Maxi-Ruthiai track under the West-Central Railway (WCR), the only main line route of my Lok Sabha constituency Rajgarh, Central Prussia, has been completed by the Government of India and the introduction of the entire track is currently underway. With the doubling of Indore, Maksi, Ruthiyai, Guna, Shivapuri, Gwanior track, a direct, cheapest and additional rail route and shortest distance rail route can be created between "Delhi Mumbai". This will facilitate the movement of passengers from Delhi directly to Mabuei via Gwanior, Shivpuri, Gannu, Kubraj, Chachdaura-Binaag, Biaora, Pachor, Sarag Paru, Maksi, Indore. Doubling will provide an additional fast track route for inter-state freight transportation to the agricultural products of the region in Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh and Maharashtra, which will give a new impetus to the industrial development of the region and will also provide additional income to the railways from commercial, medical, tourist and other passenger transport. Therefore, in public interest, please approve and promote the doubling work of Maksi, Ruthiyai, Ganu, Shivapuri, Gwaniyar tracks from Indore to meet the pressing needs of Rajgarh sub-district. (Inat) Re: Need to take comprehensive measures to check air pollution in Solapur, Maharashtra SUSHRI PRANITI SUSHILKUMAR SHINDE (SOLAPUR): Under the National Clean Air Programme, the % improvement in PM 10 levels in 2024-25 with respect to FY 2017-18 was -2% for the district of Solapur in Maharashtra. Further, it has been in the NAC category, which indicates the cities that have failed to meet the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for five consecutive years. It needs to be assessed why the air quality hasn't improved in so long. Steps need to be taken to ensure that these problems are worked out and there is an improvement in the air quality. It is of utmost need that the funds released and used are each increased in Solapur, so that the levels of PM10 and other pollutants are decreased in the air. This is essential to make the place conducive for proper respiratory health and well being of the people in Solapur. I urge the Government to take immediate steps to ensure that the people of Solapur can live a life free of harmful pollutants in the air to lead a life of good health. Re: Alleged illegal mining in Tura Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Saleng A. Sangma — Tura(St)

Speaker: Rahul Kaswan — Churu
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the government towards the extremely alarming situation arising in the agricultural sector of the state including Rajasthan, especially Churu. Recently, in a controversial report published, it has come to light that dangerous levels of pesticides and chemical elements have been found in the blood tests of farmers. Testing of 19 farmers revealed the presence of substances like organophosphate, pythroid, glyphosate, which are believed to be responsible for headaches, nerve damage, hormonal imbalance and long-term health harm. This is not the white side of the situation, but a frightening symptom of the poison mite in the farmer's body. The impact of excessive chemicals on water, soil and environment is already increasing in the desert area of ​​Rajasthan. Farmers are continuously using banned or high-toxic chemicals due to the compulsion of higher production, the results of which are now directly visible in the human body. Hence, it is requested that the government should conduct prompt medical intervention and health survey, issue new SOP for chemical use, announce special packages for safe farming, IPM and bio-alternatives. This is an emerging public health crisis. Timely action of the government is mandatory. (Inat) Re: Need for inquiry into reported theft of gold in Sabarimala Ayyappa Temple in Kerala

Speaker: Aaditya Yadav — Badaun
Speech: I would like to draw your attention towards the construction of Ganga Expressway in North Prussia and make you aware that truckloads of soil are used in the construction of expressways. This soil is obtained from the surrounding villages. Due to digging of soil, pond like pits have been formed at many places in the village. Any incident can happen at any time due to water filling in these pits during the rainy season. The village road leading to Ganga Expressway gets destroyed due to soil erosion. Roads get completely broken, there are potholes in the road and it is not even known whether there are potholes on the road or there is a road in the potholes. I draw the attention of the government to the villages of Bibanujhya, Raipur, Gudiya, Shahpuru, Kaprua, Rudra Naga, Baira, Dhara, Kaser, Bagarpur, Kania, Barin, Pastor, Karkatpur, Ranet, Veerampru, Pathakpur, Tehpur, Nasirtoa, Nasipur Kaithori, in the subsidy area of Badayu. I want to draw the attention of Behta that due to the construction of Ganga Expressway, all the roads here have been completely broken due to dust of soil, there are potholes on all four sides. When it rains, the surroundings get filled with water, as if the storm is waiting to strike. I would like to urge the Hon'ble Minister of Road Transport and Highways to include in the construction of Ganga Expressway the cities of Bibanujhaya, Raipur, Guruniya, Shahpuru, Kaprua, Rudra Nagai Beora, Dhara, Kaser, Bagarpuru, Kanipa, Baranin, Pastor, Karkatpuru, Ranet, Veerampru, Pathakpuru, Please make efforts to inform all the efforts to get the roads which have been broken due to the dust of soil which has been washed away from the villages of Tatehaparu, Nasirtoa, Nasiparu, Kaithori, Behta etc., repaired. (Inat) Re: Need to address the problems being faced by in-service teachers due to mandatory Teachers Eligibility Test as a service condition

Speaker: Saugata Roy — Dum Dum
Speech: Rupees sharp fall in recent days and the currency’s exchange rate breaking past the 90-per-dollar mark for the first time. After flirting with the 90-per-dollar mark on Tuesday, rupee finally crossed the key psychological level on Wednesday to hit another fresh all-time low. So far this year, the rupee has fallen by over 5 percent against the US dollar and is the worst-performing Asian currency. Market experts citing the continued delay in the conclusion of a free trade agreement with the US as the primary reason. Since last August, Indian goods have faced a cumulative tariff of 50 per cent while entering the world’s largest economy. India’s merchandise exports were down 12 per cent year-on year in October and the trade deficit ballooned to an all-time high of $41.7 billion. The Reserve Bank of India also seemingly loosened its grip over the exchange rate over the last year or so and intervened to a lesser extent in the foreign exchange market to stem the rupee’s decline. In 2024-25, net FDI inflows into India stood at a mere $959 million. I urge upon the Government to take immediate steps to raise the value of Indian Rupee compared to US Dollar. (ends) Re: Need to ensure disbursal of allocated funds to West Bengal under Jal Jeevan Mission SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): I wish to raise the urgent issue of the Central Government's reduction and delayed release of funds under the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) to West Bengal. The Union Government has deprived the State of its rightful dues of over Rs 2500 crores under JJM. In August, some of my colleagues from the All India Trinamool Congress even met with the Minister of Jal Shakti. However, no action has been taken whatsoever. Allocations for the current year have been significantly reduced, leading to disruptions in ongoing drinking water projects. This sudden cut has stalled pipeline work, delayed new connections, and affected payments to implementing agencies. Districts facing water scarcity and arsenic contamination are particularly impacted, putting lakhs of rural households at risk and threatening progress made under the Mission. I urge the Central Government to restore full funding and ensure timely disbursal so that JJM objectives are achieved, and rural communities do not suffer due to financial constraints. (ends) Re: Need to increase the moisture content of paddy and enhance the procurement quota by FCI in Tamil Nadu

Speaker: S. Murasoli — Thanjavur
Speech: There is a record level of paddy production in Tamil Nadu, particularly in the delta districts of the State during the Kharif season this year. Paddy procurement which stood at 4.81 Lakh metric tonnes during the year 2024-25 has increased to 14.11 Lakh metric tonnes during current year. This is a record level of paddy procurement in the history of Tamil Nadu during any Kuruvai (Kharif) season. In this regard, the State Government of Tamil Nadu has sent a proposal dated 8.8.2025 to the Union Government suggesting that the paddy procurement target should be fixed at 20 lakh metric tonnes during the Kharif Marketing Season (KMS) 2025- 26, by the Department of Food and Public Distribution of Government of India. But now the Department of Food and Public Distribution of Government of India has fixed the target for paddy procurement up to 31.3.2026 as 16 lakh metric tonnes. In view of this record level of paddy harvest and paddy production, I urge upon the Union Government to allow Tamil Nadu Government to revise and increase the total paddy procurement as per their requirement during the last quarter of the current Kharif season even though the Union Government has fixed the procurement of paddy for Kharif Marketing Season (KMS) of 2025-26 as 16 lakhs metric tonnes. Keeping in view of the rains due to the ongoing North East monsoon in the Cauvery delta and other districts of Tamil Nadu, the moisture content of paddy meant for procurement should be fixed at 22% instead of the existing 17%. Moreover the State Government should be allowed to increase the moisture content of paddy, while procurement, as per the requirement. Keeping in view, the livelihood of the farmers, I therefore urge upon the Union Government to accede to the request of the Hon. Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu in this regard. Re: Need to review the decision to spend 60% of the budget allocated under MGNREGS in the first half of the financial year.

Speaker: K. E. Prakash — Erode
Speech: Hon'ble Speaker Sir, I stand here today to indict this Government for systematically dismantling the MGNREGA. This is not inefficiency; it is a calculated attack on the rural poor. This threatens to destabilize the rural economy. The core problem is fiscal strangulation. The allocated ₹86,000 crore is fundamentally inadequate, and the newly imposed rule that only 60% of this budget may be spent in the first half of the year violates the very nature of a “work on demand” law. This cap ensures funds run out early, forcing officials to deny work or delay wages. The Government’s claim that lower demand due to a good monsoon justifies low spending ignores the reality that huge amounts are consumed merely clearing old pending bills proof of chronic underfunding. Simultaneously, digital measures meant for transparency are being weaponized for exclusion. The aggressive e-KYC push led to the deletion of nearly 27 lakh worker names in a single month. If the Government can extend 150 workdays to Jammu and Kashmir to address specific regional needs, the same flexibility must extend nationwide. I urge the Government to revoke the 60% limit, fix digital barriers, and preserve MGNREGA as a simple, reliable safety net for all. Re: Need to address the problems of in-service teachers due to mandatory Teacher Eligibility Test as a service condition

Speaker: Putta Mahesh Kumar — Eluru
Speech: The recent Supreme Court judgment mandating that all in-service teachers must clear the Teacher Eligibility Test (TET) within two years has created fear and uncertainty among lakhs of teachers across the country. When the Right to Education Act and related rules were framed, teachers appointed prior to 23 August 2010 were clearly exempted from the requirement of passing TET. Since then our teachers, some with 15 to 30 years of service have become the backbone of our schooling system. These are experienced educators who have contributed to shaping generations of students. Forcing them to take an entry-level examination at this stage of their career has hurt them deeply and frustration. The consequences of this judgment will extend far beyond teachers. If even a fraction of them are unable to clear the examination within the stipulated time, we may see thousands of classrooms become teacher-less. Therefore, I urge the Government of India to: consider suitable amendments to the Right to Education Act and the NCTE Act, 1993 to ensure that teachers who were already in service on 23 August 2010 are duly protected; and hold immediate meetings with teacher associations to take proper action to safeguard the interests of the teachers. Re: Need to expedite allocation of budget for revival of Nalanda University.

Speaker: Kaushalendra Kumar — Nalanda
Speech: To take advantage of the ancient historical, educational and cultural importance of NADA, the Budget announcement of General Budget July, 2024 proposes to revive NADA University to its former glory along with comprehensive development of the historical monuments and Rajgir there. Rajgir is a very important pilgrimage site for followers of Hinduism, Buddhism and Jainism. Rajgir is recognized as a holy place. The remains of the ancient Nada University in Nada are a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This is a matter of pride for us. Naida University, established in the 5th century, used to be the center of knowledge of the world till the 12th century. If development is done in a planned manner in NADA, then students and scholars from all over the world will come there, people will also come for tourism and tourists will be attracted to the rich historical and cultural heritage of NADA and there will also be employment opportunities there. Therefore, the Honorable Minister is requested to please draft the proposal for budget announcement as soon as possible. Speaker: R. Sachidanandam — Dindigul
Speech: The Pulayan community (except Kanyakumari district & Sengottai taluk of Tirunelveli District) has been listed at sl.no. 2(18) in the Scheduled Tribes list of Tamil Nadu. As per the SC & ST orders(Amendment) Act 1976 (Central act No. 108 of 1976), the Pulayan community has been listed at sl. no.59 in the SC list of Tamil Nadu. The Anthropological observation from field inquiry the data analysis reveals the interpretations that the community studied residing at Tirupur, coimbatore and Dindigul district, Tamil Nadu have Tribal characteristics. Re: Need for a statutory urban employment guarantee policy on the lines of the MGNREGA to address unemployment in urban areas.

Speaker: Vishal Patil — Sangli
Speech: I wish to draw the attention of the Government to the absence of an Employment Guarantee Scheme for Urban Areas. Data from the Periodic Labor Force Survey shows that urban unemployment remains higher than rural unemployment, with urban unemployment rates around 6.5- 7% and unemployment rates among youth and urban females far higher, reflecting a deep structural jobs crisis in city areas. The absence of a statutory employment guarantee in urban areas leaves migrant workers, construction labourers, domestic workers, street vendors and gig workers without a basic livelihood floor during economic shocks, pandemics, or downturns in demand. Rising living costs in cities further aggravate distress, pushing vulnerable households into debt and informal, low-productivity work. I therefore urge the Hon’ble Minister of Rural Development to address the need for a statutory Urban Employment Guarantee on the lines of the MGNREGA to provide at least 100 days of demand-driven wage employment to urban households engaged in the informal sector, prioritizing women and youth, and linked to creation and maintenance of urban public assets and services such as drainage, waste management, urban greening and climate-resilience interventions and allow rural registered workers to opt for. notified works within city limits. (ends) Discussion on electoral reforms - continued 1212 hours. Honorable Chairman: Shri K.C. Vengu oppa.

Speaker: K. C. Venugopal — Alappuzha
Speech: Thank you very much, hon. Speaker, Sir, for giving me the opportunity to participate in this debate on electoral reforms, a matter both important and crucial for our democracy. I would like to recall the words of the architect of our Constitution, Dr. B. R. Ambedkar: “The House will realize that franchise is the most fundamental thing in a democracy. No person who is entitled to be brought into the electoral rolls on the grounds which we have already mentioned in our Constitution, namely, an adult of 21 years of age should be excluded merely as a result of the prejudice of a local Government, or the whim of an officer. That would cut at the very root of democratic Government.” … Honorable Speaker: No, Honorable Member, please. These historical words from Dr. B. R. Ambedkar, spoken on June 15th, 1949, when Article 289 was introduced, were not merely a warning; they were a constitutional prophecy. For decades following the first general election of 1951–52, the Election Commission enjoyed the full confidence of the nation. But unfortunately today, that sanctity has been completely compromised. The idea of ​​an impartial electoral umpire has now been replaced. It now openly collapses under political pressure and turns into a party sign. The right to vote is not a favor granted by the Government. This is the fundamental principle of democracy. Every citizen above 18 is guaranteed a right. As the hon. Leader of the Opposition yesterday pointed out, vote theft is the act. Unfortunately, instead of preventing this act, the Election Commission has been enabling it. Sir, I brought it to your notice before the general elections 2024. Our Congress Party does not have much resources in its kitty. We are a poor party in this country, not like BJP. What happened just before the general elections? Our entire account was frozen. What was the reason? The reason was delay in filing our Party's Income Tax Return for two weeks. Can you imagine in a democratic world that a political party’s account is frozen due to delay in filing Income Tax Return, that too just before the general elections? This was their attitude. We filed a petition with the Election Commission. They did not intervene at all. You can see that whenever an election is going to be announced, Income Tax Department becomes active, CBI is active, ED is active and there are raids after raids against the Opposition. This is not happening to the BJP people. They are completely free. I am bringing to your notice two occasions. We had been reading the deliberations and discussions that happened in the Constituent Assembly on the issue of Election Commission. At that point itself, this question arose. Since 1950, the Election Commission has commanded the trust of the entire nation. There were no serious allegations against the Election Commission at all. But in recent years, many petitions have been filed in the Supreme Court of India. At that point of time, the Supreme Court constituted a Constitution Bench under Justice K.M. Joseph. The Constitution Bench led by Justice K.M. Joseph gave a verdict that until a law is enacted, there should be a mechanism including the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition of Lok Sabha and the Chief Justice of Supreme Court. In the Anoop Baranwal versus Union of India case, this judgment was given by the Constitution Bench led by Justice K.M. Joseph that until Parliament legislated, the CEC and EC must be appointed by a committee comprising the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition and the Chief Justice of India. The goal was very simple, Sir. The intention of the Supreme Court was very simple that there should be an impartial empire which can do free and fair election for Election Commission of India. This was the intention of the Supreme Court. Nobody can question the intention of the Supreme Court. Yesterday, the hon. Minister told us that they made a law because the Supreme Court directed them. Whenever you are making a law, at least you should apply your mind as to what the intention of the Supreme Court's verdict is. The Supreme Court's verdict is very clear that there should be an impartial empire for conducting elections. But you did not want the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court in that committee. Immediately after the Supreme Court's judgment, you enacted that law, removing the Chief Justice from that committee. … Your intention is very clear. You are very much scared of putting the Chief Justice of India in that committee. The Chief Justice is not our man. …The Chief Justice is going to be a neutral man. … Let me complete, brother. …Yesterday, the hon. Leader of the Opposition told about that. …

Speaker: K. C. Venugopal — Alappuzha
Speech: Sir, this was one instance. …the hon. Law Minister has to answer. You did not answer the question of LoP yesterday. You have done so many but you did not answer the real questions put by LoP and other Opposition leaders. What was the reason for making a law to give immunity to the Election Commission of India?… You did not answer it. You can verify it. The real question of this country is that till 2023 so many Election Commissions were there. Brave, neutral, dynamic Election Commissions were there. Nobody got this immunity. Nobody asked for this immunity. Why this demand of immunity for the Election Commission came now in 2023? … There is an answer, hon. Minister. It is because of the of the Election Commission and the Government. You know that. You know that you are violating the basic principle of democracy. Honorable Speaker: You address the Chair. This is impunity for the This is going to be a lifetime washing machine procedure. … Basically, this immunity is going to be an impunity for Again, I am repeating, this is an all-time washing machine. Prime Minister’s speech in Banswara, 2024. … Honorable Speaker: One minute. … (Interruptions) Honorable Speaker: It is not done like this. … (obstacle)

Speaker: Tangella Uday Srinivas — Kakinada
Speech: Hon. Chairperson, Sir, I rise to draw the attention of this August House to a vast yet under-recognized opportunity of need to urgently strengthen India’s furniture manufacturing and export ecosystem. Furniture is one of the fastest growing consumer sectors in the world and is expected to cross USD 800 billion globally. India has all the ingredients to lead this industry – a skilled workforce, good timber resource, a rich craft tradition, and strong domestic and global demand. Yet our share in global furniture exports is just one per cent, far beyond much smaller economies like Vietnam and Malaysia. Even though we are one of the largest furniture producers in the world, India still imported almost half a billion dollars worth of furniture in 2023. This clearly shows gap in our manufacturing capacity, our product range, and our global competitiveness. The challenges are well known. Nearly 85 per cent of furniture in the country is made by informal carpenters, which limits scaling up and standardization. High duties on engineered wood and fittings increase costs. We lack design centres, testing labs, and strong global branding. Our logistics, especially long distance from ports, make it harder for Indian exporters to compete. In this context, port-linked regions, like Kakinada with deep-draft facilities and land for integrated clusters, offer a major opportunity. Reducing logistics alone can significantly improve export competitiveness. This sector is labor intensive, innovation driven, and capable of creating lakhs of skilled jobs, formalizing artisans, boosting exports, and supporting timber plantations. What we need now is a focused national strategy. Therefore, I request the Union Government to consider the following: establishing mega furniture parks with common CNC facilities, seasoning yards, and port-linked logistics hubs; launching a PLI scheme for furniture manufacturing as recommended by NITI Aayog; reducing import duties on engineered wood fittings and adhesives; setting up national design and skill institute for furniture, technology and industrial prototyping; and creating a branding and export promotion mission to showcase Indian furniture globally. Through right interventions, our MSMEs can grow into global manufacturers and make India a strong and trusted player in international furniture value chain.

In Vennaimalai of Karur District, people who are living in Inam (endowment) land with proper patta (the ownership right) should be protected from being vacated on the basis of a Court Order. These are not temple lands as viewed by the Courts. These are lands which were donated to those who offered service to God since the last century. Therefore this land belongs to them. The ownership rights have been properly registered in the names of these families. Patta or the document of ownership right has been acquiredby the owners of this Inamland30years ago. They have even purchased this land by paying money in a legal manner. Registration has also been done by the government after payment of required stamp duties by them. Property tax has been levied and property owners have the receipts of this payment. Banks also had given loans to buy these properties as they possessed Patta for several generations. How can this be considered as a crime? Whether our people have constructed multi-storey buildings and having business in a large scale? They are having only residences. What will they do for their livelihood? How will they educate their children? How can they run their families? They have Patta in their names. If it is so, how can it be illegal to have small businesses? This issue doesn't only belong to Vennaimalai, Pugzhali Malai, Venjamankudalur areas but it has a larger area. The same person has filedacaseintheCourregarding20suchtemplelandsinKarurdistrict.Ifsuchasituationcontinues, KarurdistrictwhichhadnameandfameduringtheCheradynastywillcompletelydisappear.BoththeUnionandStategovernmentshavetoshoulderresponsibilityinprotectingKarurdistrict.EvenifTamil Nadu government issues Order for protection of such affected people in Karur, within 2 hours of issuance of such an Order, the Opposite parties approach the Courts and get those Orders them stayed. Eyes filledwithtears andsorrows of ourpeoplearegivingussomuchpain. With introduction of one particular bill, the State of Kashmir has been changed onto a Union Territory. Can't government find a solution to the affected people of Vennaimalai? This issue has further aggravated and as much as 13 lakh acres of temple land throughout Tamil Nadu is under threat. Several thousands of people have been facing unbearable sorrow and suffering. In consultation with State government, I urge that the Union government should bring a legislation to protect the houses and livelihoodofpeopleofTamilNaduparticularlylyofVennaimalaiinKarurdistrict.Thankyou.

In November, 2019, I had written to the Minister of Road Transport and Highways and the Minister of Housing and Urban Affairs that there is a need to connect these four cities and build a mass transit transit system in the system, so that the economic potential of this entire region can be fully realized. I had proposed that now a mass transit bridge should be built in Poha Sisth from Waha to Kurari and from Dahra to Pijdaur. On top of that, the government constituted a Unified Metropolitan Transit Authority, but the fortunate thing is that the Unified Metropolitan Transit Authority has met only three times since it was constituted. He said that he had asked the Economic Authority (RIGHT) to submit a comprehensive report on whether a metro can be built for these four cities or not? Rice reported twice that yes, a metro is necessary to connect these four cities and there is a need to build a metro. But no further action was taken against him. In the last three-four years, the cost of that project has gradually increased from Rs 16 thousand crore to Rs 25 thousand crore. Madam, Metro was built in Nagpur, Metro was built in Kochi, Metro was built in Bagiru, Metro was built in Hyderabad and Metro was also built in Membui. Metro has been built in all the cities, but metro has not been built in Chittigarh for the last 10 years. It is my specific request to the Central Government that the Government should adopt it under the Strategic Connectivity Project and allocate Rs 25,000 crore from the Central Government, so that this entire region can be integrated and its economic potential can be channelized. Thank you very much. *SHRI KOTA SRINIVASA POOJARY (UDUPI CHIKMAGALUR): Hon'ble Chairpeson, Sir, There are complaints that lakhs of farmers have not received their crop insurance payments. On behalf of all those farmers, I urge the central government in this regard. In my state of Karnataka, there has been an allegation that in Chikkamagalur district alone, there are 39,000 farmers were not received their crop insurance. Sir, in some places, *OriginalinKannada there are complaint that the farmers were not received even the premium amount paid by them for the crop insurance. I urge the central government through you that there are complaints about the rain gauges installed in the rural areas are damaged. Farmers have been vocally expressing their annoyance that they must turn to consumer courts in order to receive the premium amount they have paid. Sir, a scenario has developed in which farmers from the Chikmagalur, Tarikere, Mudigere, and Sringeri constituencies have taken to streets for protesting as there were ill-treated by the insurance companies and causing inordinate delay to make the payment of crop insurance. caus claiming they have been treated unfairly due to the delayed release of crop insurance. It is not only of Shivamogga and Uttara Kannada districts, but farmers from all over the state are agitating over the delay caused by the insurance companies in releasing the payment of crop insurance. Hence, I urge upon the hon’ble Union Minister for Agriculture through you to sought a report from the Chief Secretary of the Government of Karnataka and also the Deputy Commissioners of all the 31 districts of the state to ensure that the complete sum of crop insurance is disbursed without further delay.

Speaker: Chintamani Maharaj — Surguja(St)
Speech: Hon'ble Speaker Sir, I would like to draw the attention of my friend Sir to a serious public need of Surguja division. Avamkapur - Hazrat Vanjameddu Green Express, Train No. 22407 is the only direct train service from Surguja to Delhi which currently runs once a week. Only one word is true. Thousands of students, graduates, working people and businessmen from Surguja, Birampur, Surajpur and Koriya districts visit Delhi regularly. Due to limited information they have to face problems like lack of money, waiting lists and having to resort to alternative means. This situation is in stark contrast to the development needs of this emerging region. Honorable Speaker Sir, I request my friend to run Avambakapur-Hazrat Vanjameddu Green Express, Train No. 22407 at least twice a week, so that the common people can get real exposure to it and regional connectivity can be strengthened. Thank you. Honorable Chairman (Shri Krishna Prasad Tennati): Honorable Dr. Shatabdi Roy. Ma'am, you can express your views in two and a half minutes. *SHRIMATISATABDIROYBANERJEE(BIRBHUM):Honorablespeaker,ThankyouSir. Today I am drawing the attention of this House to a very painful and thought-provoking reality. MBBS-MD students who are pursuing medical education have to work for at least 36 hours. The unbearable work pressure put on them is beyond human limits. In most of the hospitals in the country, these young doctors have to work for 36 to 48 hours continuously without any rest. This is unpleasant to hear but, this is their professional reality. In other fields, the work limit of 8 to 12 hours is followed, so why inhuman rules for these doctors who are pursuing MBBS-MD? A common man cannot even drive a vehicle safely after staying awake for 24 hours, because the result is fatigue, restlessness, stress. On the other hand, the doctors who are in the medical field provide continuous medical services despite lack of sleep, mental pressure, physical fatigue. This is an injustice to their lives and safety. A tired doctor not only endangers himself but also the patients who come to him for medical treatment. When doctors get tired, the entire health system gets tired. When doctors collapse, the entire medical system collapses. Today, I request the House that we need to consider the lives, health, and safety of these young doctors seriously.The 36 to 48-hour shifts they are doing should be stopped immediately and they should be given an 8-hour shifts so that their work can be limited. This is my humble request to you that the injustice done to doctors should be stopped immediately. Thank you.

I would like to draw the attention of the government towards the serious fact that there are lakhs of teachers who have served for twenty-twenty-five thirty years and became teachers only after fulfilling all the standards of the previous government. Now they have also been forced to pass the TET exam, which is not fair. Chairman, this decision is impractical and inhumane for the senior teacher and is administratively inefficient. Due to such decisions, lakhs of teachers and their families have come to Matukkal and some teachers have committed suicide in Badula Khod. I request the Education Minister to exempt senior teachers from TET exam by bringing in more teachers. Advocate Chandra Shekhar (Nagina): Mr. Chairman, I would like to draw your attention to the very important but long neglected issue of ‘Menstrual Holiday’ related to crores of working women. Sir, menstruation is not a disease but a biological process, but despite all the pain, weakness and health complications that occur during it, millions of women laborers, daughters of farmers, girl students, office workers and women from unorganized sectors are in this situation without any reason. Forced to do slaughter work. Even today there is no clear national policy regarding monthly religious holidays in our country. Some private institutions and two or three states are taking initiative, but this step is yet to be implemented nationwide. I urge the government to prepare a national policy on monthly religious holidays. Women of Asag Thathi area should also be included in this and necessary provisions should also be made for school-college girls. Extensive surveys, research, studies and consultation processes should be initiated on this subject. Women's dignity, health and equal opportunities cannot be ignored in any modern society. The time has come when Indians also accept the monthly religious holiday as a right. Jai Tahad, Jai Bharati.

Speaker: N. K. Premachandran — Kollam
Speech: Thank you very much Mr. Chairman Sir. Sir, I would like to draw the immediate attention of the Ministry of Road, Transport and Highways regarding the construction of National Highway - 66 which is passing through my constituency. On 5th December, 2025, in my constituency, near to Mylakkadu – Kottiyam section on National Highway-66 which is under construction, the road has totally collapsed causing huge damages. The reason for the accident is unscientific design, alignment and construction of the highway. This is not an isolated incident. A series of incidents has happened in Alappuzha, Thiruvananthapuram and the Malabar parts of the State also. Last month, two people have died – one passenger and one worker. The accidents are happening in places where national highway and service roads are separated by huge retaining walls filled with soil. There are 30 to 40 feet height of earthen retaining walls at various places. This is a threat to the safety of the roads. All these works are done without conducting any scientific studies, without taking into consideration the topography, climatic conditions and the characteristics of the road. A series of similar accidents has established the fact that the elevated road with earthen retaining wall is not safe and that is to be dropped. Hence, I make to the hon. The Minister and the Government made the following two demands, while saying that at these places, elevated concrete highway pillars are required. Number one is to conduct a high level investigation at the places of accidents and a scientific study regarding the safety of the elevated highway; and earthen retaining wall has to be done away with. Second one is to construct concrete elevated highway on pillars in the place of earthen retaining walls which are having huge height. Speaker: Dean Kuriakose — Idukki
Speech: Sir, the primary amoebic disease popularly known as the brain eating amoeba has spread all over Kerala at an alarming rate. What was once considered a rare disease globally has now turned into a nightmare for families in Kerala. In the last few months alone, we have seen over 60 confirmed cases and nearly 20 tragic deaths, mostly children and teenagers. The fatality rate is over 90 percent. Sir, scientific reports indicate that climate change is warming our freshwater bodies, turning them into breeding grounds for this lethal amoeba. This is no longer just a State subject, it is a climate-driven biological disaster. While the State Government is doing its best with a 'One Health' approach, we desperately need the Centre's support. I demand the Union Health Ministry to dispatch a Central Expert Team from NCDC immediately to study why Kerala has become a hotspot for this communicable disease, ensure the supply of life-saving drugs, and direct the ICMR to initiate urgent research to conduct a study.

For the successful implementation of this transformative initiative, I urge upon the Union Government to extend its support by making adequate financial provisions. Such an assistance will not only strengthen the infrastructure of public learning but also empower citizens with the tools of knowledge which is essential for national progress.

Speaker: Umeshbhai Patel — Daman And Diu
Speech: Sir, through you I would like to draw the attention of the Government to the grave condition of our Indian fisherman lodged in Pakistani jail. The condition of imprisoned fishermen in Pakistan is very pathetic. Many of these fishermen are suffering from serious illness. Among the fishermen jailed in Pakistan, most of them have completed their jail sentence. We had raised the issue on this issue about a year ago and we were given assurance, but till date no action has been taken. In this regard, I request the Government of India to ensure the immediate release of all these fishermen and ensure their return to their native land and to prevent such unjustified arrests in future, concrete steps should be taken such as clear identification of maritime boundaries, mandatory use of GPS devices, continuous monitoring of the Coast Guard. The Government of India is extremely responsible for the Indian fishermen and I request the Government of India to take action on this issue as soon as possible.

Farmers' maize is being sold in the market for Rs 1,000 to Rs 1,200 per quintal, due to which the farmer is not even able to meet his expenses. So: It is very important to purchase maize at minimum price. This government will provide economic security to the hard work of the farmers by providing them good prices at regular intervals and will help in keeping the income of the farmers stable, which will help in growing maize and sugarcane in future. Sir, apart from this, honorable Modi ji has planned 'Shri-Anna', there will be encouragement for that. The government can use the maize purchased at MSP to serve purposes like food supply as well as ethanol production. In Sugar Tamil, the additional deduction being made from sugarcane farmers should be changed and the time limit for Bhaghu Khan should be fixed. I am also thankful to the Central Prussian Government, which has reduced the price on soybean, and I am also thankful to both the Central Government and the State Government for purchasing Moong. Also, I express my gratitude to the Honorable Chief Minister for giving Rs. 4 thousand per hectare on land. The surplus quantity of gram and wheat should be purchased at MSP in future. Nyayavaad.

Speaker: Karti Chidambaram — Sivaganga
Speech: Hon. Chairperson Sir, on November 30th, there was a head on collision between two State Transport Corporation buses at a place called Nachiapuram, which is in my constituency, Sivaganga. About 12 people died, including both the drivers, and forty people were injured. One of them was a young engineering student named Diana, who belonged to Economically Weaker Section. Sir, this is not a rare occurrence. In India, we have more than 1,72,000 deaths in road accidents annually. In fact, this statistics is from 2023. We do not have the current day statistics. This means that about 461 people in India die every day in road accidents, which is akin to almost having two air crashes every day. When one air crash occurred, there was such a big news. But there are two air crashes happening on our roads every day. There are many reasons why these accidents happen, such as head on collisions, over-speeding, non-compliance with the rules, and poor infrastructure. One of the main reasons is the skill deficit of drivers. Sir, through you, I would like to request the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways to strengthen the norms under which licenses are issued. More tests must be conducted, and this liberal approach to granting licenses should stop. We need to implement eye tests, reflex tests, and also assess whether there is any kind of substance abuse among these drivers. We must strengthen these protocols. Our country cannot afford to have more than two lakh deaths annually in road accidents. Thank you very much.

Speaker: Sanjay Jaiswal — Paschim Champaran

Thanks. SHRIMATI SANGEETA KUMARI SINGH DEO (BOLANGIR): Thank you, Sir. I wish to raise an issue of urgent importance affecting cotton farmers across Western Odisha, especially in the aspirational and drought-prone districts of Balangir, Kalahandi, Nuapada, Rayagada, and Subarnapur. Odisha contributes significantly to the State's cotton acreage, yet the value chain is currently under severe distress, due to procedural restrictions imposed by the Cotton Corporation of India (CCI). This year, CCI has introduced a new rule which forces farmers to sell their produce only in the district of cultivation, restricting their access to nearby districts with more ginning units and smoother unloading facilities. This has increased transportation burden, waiting time, and has led to under-utilization of several units while others face overwhelming rush. Further, the compulsory mobile app-based registration, through the Kapas Kisan App has created some hurdles. In many cotton growing belts, a large number of farmers belong to the tribal community, with limited digital literacy and poor internet access. As a result, farmer registrations remain extremely low, and procurement has been delayed. Hon. CHAIRPERSON (SHRI DILIP SAIKIA): Madam, please raise your demands. SHRIMATI SANGEETA KUMARI SINGH DEO (BOLANGIR): I urge the Government to direct CCI to remove district-wise barriers, allowing farmers to sell at any convenient procurement centre, and permit both online and offline registration during this introduction year. Thank you, Sir.

Speaker: Vijay Vasanth — Kanyakumari
Speech: Thank you, Sir. I rise to highlight the critical issues affecting the fishing communities of Kanyakumari District. The Thengapattanam Fishing Harbor safety crisis, lack of reliable deep-sea communication systems, and inadequate marine conservation frameworks are just a few of the major challenges they face. The fundamental difference between fishing and farming is often overlooked in laws and regulations leading to conflicts and livelihood insecurity. It is imperative that we recognize the unique socio-economic and ecological realities of fishermen. I would urge the Minister to form a Joint High-Level Expert Committee comprising marine scientists, socio-economic and legal experts, coastal MPs, and local stakeholders. This Committee should conduct a detailed study on fishing and farming livelihoods; review existing marine laws and regulations; recommend science-based participatory reforms for sustainable livelihoods; and formulate a long-term roadmap for safety, conservation, and economic development. The Committee's recommendations will help to protect the livelihoods of coastal communities and safeguarding marine ecosystems.

Speaker: Yaduveer Wadiyar — Mysore
Speech: Sir, I rise to draw the urgent attention of this August House to the increasing threat confronting India’s heritage cities, especially my own constituency of Mysore. We are witnessing unregulated, unscientific, and insensitive construction and urbanization carried out with complete disregard for the architectural and historical character of these cities. This situation has largely arisen because we lack a forward-looking urban framework tailored for heritage cities. Through you, I urge upon the Ministry of Urban Affairs to formulate a dedicated heritage urban development framework for cities like Mysore. Such a framework must include mandatory heritage impact assessments, stronger building codes and zoning regulations, expert-led heritage conservation committees, assured funding along with PPP models as well as digital mapping and comprehensive heritage inventories. All these measures are essential as we move ahead. They will help us safeguard not only our urban settlements but also our civilizational repositories that hold our culture and collective memory, particularly in Mysore. Thank you.

Speaker: Ajendra Singh Lodhi — Hamirpur
Speech: I wish to highlight the urgent matter of desiltation of major reservoirs in Himachal Pradesh. The Bhakra Beas Management Board (BBMB) has repeatedly submitted proposals for desiltation, but key permissions and files have reportedly been withheld by the Himachal Pradesh Government, delaying essential restoration work. Over the years, heavy sediment influx from the Sutlej and Beas rivers has substantially reduced reservoir capacity. Bhakra Reservoir has lost over 20-25% of its original storage, while Pong Reservoir has recorded annual sedimentation rates of 12-14 million cubic metres, cumulatively reducing its live storage by nearly one-third. Similar trends are visible in Kol and other upstream barrages. This rapid silt accumulation has already impacted irrigation releases to Punjab, Haryana and Rajasthan, reduced dependable hydropower generation, and weakened the flood-moderation ability of these dams. If desiltation is not undertaken immediately, the reservoirs will continue to lose capacity every year, undermining the very purpose for which these national assets were constructed. In view of the seriousness of the situation, I urge the Ministry of Power, Ministry of Jal Shakti and the (BBMB) to work with the Himachal Pradesh Government and fast-track a time-bound desiltation program Bhakra, PNG and other major Reservoirs. Immediate action is essential to restore reservoir capacity, safeguard infrastructure, and ensure that these nationally critical multipurpose projects continue to serve their intended functions effectively. Speaker: Kamakhya Prasad Tasa — Kaziranga
Speech: I wish to draw the attention of this House to an urgent and important matter concerning Assam's textile and handloom sector-a sector that is not just an economic lifeline, but also a living expression of country's cultural identity. Assam is home to some of India's finest handloom traditions-Muga, Eri, Pat silk, tribal weaves, and beautifully crafted cotton products. Over 15 lakh weavers, most of them women, rely on this sector for their livelihood. Yet, despite its rich potential, the industry continues to face serious hurdles: a shortage of modern processing facilities, limited availability of quality raw materials, weak market linkages, and the lack of integrated textile parks with shared infrastructure. Our rural artisans are battling rising production costs, outdated equipment, and insufficient digital access to wider national and international markets. Sir, with the right support, Assam's textile and handloom sector can become a major engine for jobs, women's empowerment, export growth, and cultural outreach. To unlock this, I humbly urge the Government to establish a Mega Integrated Textile Park in Assam under PM MITRA to provide world-class infrastructure and attract investors; set up a National Research and Innovation Center to promote scientific cultivation, quality enhancement, and global branding; provide direct marketing support, e-commerce on boarding, and export promotion assistance to weavers and cooperatives; Introduce a special credit and insurance package for women weavers and small handloom clusters and strengthen raw material banks, dyeing units, common facility centres, and skill-upgradation institutes across the state. Sir, a dedicated national push can transform Assam's textile heritage into a robust economic powerhouse. I request the Government to address this matter with priority. Serious issues like drugs, environmental pollution and migration have become a threat to Punjab and Punjab's society. I request the government to give us a special package of ₹ 70,000 crore to save Punjab which is called Khadag Puja and Granary of India. (Iti) Re: Construction of rail overbridges/underpasses at various level crossings in Karur and Tiruchirappalli districts of Tamil Nadu SUSHRI S. JOTHIMANI (KARUR): I rise to bring to the attention of the Government an urgent public infrastructure requirement concerning Karur District (Salem Railway Division) and Manapparai Assembly area in Tiruchirappalli District (Madurai Railway Division). Road Over Bridges (ROBs) / Subways are urgently required at the following level crossing locations in Karur District due to heavy traffic congestion, frequent gate closures, and increasing public safety concerns: Noyyal – Velayuthampalayam, Punnam Chatram – TNPL, Maravapalayam – Gandhi Nagar, Mayanur, Veerarakkiyam. Similarly, the following railway gates under the Madurai Division in the Manapparai Assembly Constituency require immediate sanction for ROB/Subway construction due to high vehicular movement and accident risk: Elangakurichi Railway Gate, Vyampatti Railway Station Gate. These level crossings severely disrupt daily commuting, delay emergency vehicles, affect school and office timings, and hinder the movement of agricultural produce in these predominantly rural and semi-urban regions. I request the Hon'ble Railway Minister to immediately direct the concerned Railway Divisions to undertake feasibility studies, approve Detailed Project Reports (DPRs), and sanction the construction of ROBs/Subways at the above locations at the earliest, in the interest of public safety and smooth transport connectivity. Re: Formation of a National Anti Fake Drugs Task Force to combat counterfeit cough syrups and fake drugs

Speaker: Rajeev Kumar Rai — Ghosi
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the Government to a very serious and sensitive public health crisis – the tragic deaths of innocent children who lost their lives due to poisonous and spurious cough syrups. Alarming information has come to light about a widespread and widespread racket of fake cough syrup and other illegal drugs being active in many states of Central Prussia, Rajasthan, West Bengal, Bihar and Jharkhand region along with the North Eastern region. This has resulted in the untimely death of hundreds of children, which is extremely painful and heart-breaking. This is not only a matter of law and order, but is also a direct threat to the health safety of the population. To curb counterfeit products, better coordination between the Central and State Governments, strict monitoring system by DCGI, regular and transparent checking of pharmacy manufacturing and supply chain is essential. Therefore, I urge the government to conduct a high-level, time-bound investigation of this entire network, take strict action against the perpetrators and constitute an “Anti-Fake Drugs and Toxic Force” at the national level to prevent such incidents in future. (Iti) Re: Extension of Delhi-Meerut Rapid Rail service to Muzaffarnagar and Haridwar

Speaker: Jagadish Chandra Barma Basunia — Cooch Behar(Sc)
Speech: The Cooch Behar Royal Palace (Rajbari) is an important part of North Bengal's rich cultural heritage. The 1886-built European style palace has been functioning as a museum since 2000 under the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and attracts over 2000 visitors each day. Concernily, while the tourist footfall and revenue generated have been increasing steadily, the maintenance grant from the ASI has not kept up with this. For example, between 2021-22 and 2023-24, both the number of visitors and revenue generated increased by 290%. However, the ASI maintenance grant during the same period shrank by more than 13%. This has directly translated into poor upkeep of the palace, shortage of staff and inconvenience to visitors. Basic infrastructure like public toilets and rest areas, drinking water points and signages are poorly maintained. The shortage of staff has led to 56 of the 64 palace rooms remaining closed to the public while the two folk cultural galleries have remained shut for years. It also severely compromises cleanliness and tourist satisfaction. Hence, I urge the Government to intervene immediately to restore the Cooch Behar Palace to its past glory and approve a special heritage project for it under the Ministry of Culture. Re: Need to take steps to address the problems being faced by handloom weavers in Tamil Nadu

Speaker: S. Jagathrakshakan — Arakkonam
Speech: I wish to raise a matter of urgent public importance regarding the growing distress among handloom weavers in Tamil Nadu. The handloom sector is one of the oldest and most culturally significant industries in the State, providing livelihood to over 4 lakh weaver households and supporting many more through allied activities. Tamil Nadu’s renowned products—Kanchipuram silk sarees, Madurai Sungadi, Chettinad cotton and Salem dhoties—reflect exceptional craftsmanship and sustain rural communities. However, the sector is facing serious challenges. Rising competition from power looms, cheap machine-made fabrics, limited access to modern technology and weak marketing opportunities have reduced incomes and created uncertainty for artisans. Many weavers work long hours under physically demanding conditions, often leading to chronic health issues. Welfare schemes intended for their benefit frequently fail to reach them due to procedural delays, poor awareness and weak implementation also. The GST policy favors KVIC khadi with full exemption, while handloom weavers still face 5% GST, putting Tamil Nadu's handloom sector at an unfair disadvantage. I urge the Union Government to take immediate steps to strengthen the handloom ecosystem by improving credit access, expanding market linkages, also to waive the GST from the handloom weaver sector. Re: Establishment of Head Post Office at Jhanjharpur market in Madhubani district, Bihar

Speaker: Omprakash Rajenimbalkar — Osmanabad
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the Government to the very worrying condition of the equipment used in relief and rescue operations during the recent floods in different areas of the country. The boats, life jackets, communication equipment, rafters and other equipment currently in use are neither up to modern standards nor Available in sufficient quantity. Due to their oldness and inadequacy, rescue operations get delayed and the possibility of casualties increases. Today, the use of technologies such as drone-based surveillance, satellite communications, advanced motorboats, surface imaging equipment and high quality life-saving equipment is essential for the speed and effectiveness of rescue operations. Therefore, I urge the government to take necessary steps to equip the NDRF, SDRF, local administration and disaster management agencies with state-of-the-art equipment and technology as soon as possible. The sector contributes nearly 18% to the GDP, and 46% of the population depends on it. To address market distortions, states established APMCs to provide a regulated marketplace and ensure fair returns to farmers. However, the Pune APMC has reported repeated instances of mismanagement and malpractice, indicating a breakdown in its functioning and a distortion of its intended purpose. Multiple complaints indicate the market committee indulging in loss-making financial transactions undertaken for personal interests, irregularities in the grant of purchase and sale licenses, and long-pending applications of genuine landowners, even as permits are issued to individuals with no legitimate connection to the market. Reports of vendors illegally renting out their shops to unauthorized sellers continue, but instead of addressing farmers’ grievances, the committee appears to support such illegal practices. Additionally, there are complaints about irregularities in the permanent appointment of daily-wage workers and a scam involving non-payment of the mandated Mathadi levy. I urge the Government to intervene and ensure that APMC funds support farmer welfare and modernization of the market. Re: Need for a scientific and holistic approach to address human-leopard conflict in Pune and Ahilyanagar districts in Maharashtra

Speaker: Maddila Gurumoorthy — Tirupati(Sc)
Speech: I rise to draw the urgent attention of the House to a grave and deeply distressing crisis affecting thousands of NEET-UG aspirants in Andhra Pradesh. Under the YSR Congress administration, 17 new Government Medical Colleges were sanctioned and construction of seven was completed to ensure quality medical education for students from rural and low-income families. Yet today, only eight colleges are functioning, while several fully built institutions remain idle due to unexplained delays in regulatory approvals. Because of this inaction, over 1,000 medical seats that should have been available this year were lost, and many bright and deserving students missed their chance by the narrowest margins, not due to lack of merit but because the system failed them. To make matters worse, the move to privatize Government medical colleges raises serious fears that private managements may prioritize revenue over service, pushing economically weaker sections out of medical education and weakening affordable healthcare in underserved districts. These colleges were built with massive public funds, and the future of an entire generation now hangs in uncertainty. I strongly urge the Government to immediately clear all pending approvals and protect both student futures and equitable healthcare access for the poor. Re: Need to set up an AIIMS/PGIMER like hospital in Sri Anandpur Sahib Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Malwinder Singh Kang — Anandpur Sahib
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the house to the urgent need for the establishment of an AIIMS or PGIMER-like hospital in the Sri Anandpur Sahib constituency in Punjab. The residents of Anandpur Sahib and the surrounding rural belt face severe difficulties in accessing quality healthcare. The region lacks advanced medical facilities, specialized doctors, and essential diagnostic equipment required for the treatment of chronic and life-threatening diseases such as heart ailments, cancer, diabetes, kidney disorders, and neurological conditions. Due to the absence of such facilities, patients—especially those who are critically ill are forced to travel long distances to Chandigarh, or other major cities for proper medical treatment. This travel often takes several hours, and in many unfortunate cases, patients have lost their lives while being transported because timely medical intervention was not available locally. This situation has caused immense hardship to poor, elderly, and rural families. I therefore urge through you to the Hon'ble Minister to take immediate steps to sanction and establish an AIIMS/PGIMER-like hospital in the Sri Anandpur Sahib Parliamentary Constituency, so that people may receive high-quality healthcare within their own region and no life is lost for want of timely treatment. Speaker: K. Sudhakar — Chikballapur
Speech: Chikkaballapur and the surrounding regions stand today at the threshold of a remarkable opportunity. Blessed with sacred heritage sites such as the revered Sri Bhoga Nandeeshwara Temple, the spiritually uplifting Nandi Hills, the ancient Papagni Mutt, Subramanya Swamy Temple, and the neighboring mystical Antharagange caves, Chikkaballapur and neighboring regions are naturally poised to become one of India’s finest religious tourism destinations. Chintamani Taluk is a treasure trove of prehistoric sites. The rock paintings are believed to date back to the Neolithic period, roughly 10,000 years ago. Yet, despite attracting lakhs of pilgrims and visitors, the true potential of these treasures remains largely untapped. What we need now is a visionary push. With support under schemes like PRASHAD and Swadesh Darshan 2.0, we can build modern amenities, strengthen last-mile connectivity, create digital information centres, improve sanitation and safety infrastructure, and develop environmentally sustainable facilities that respect the sanctity of these sites. Enhanced facilities will generate employment, empower local communities, and support our artisans and farmers. Rural tourism especially village and farm tourism has huge potential in Chikkaballapur. I request the government of India to support and make the region a model domestic and global tourism destination. (Imat) Re: Need to release revised pension and arrears to EPF pensioners as per the judgment of Supreme Court

Speaker: Rajmohan Unnithan — Kasaragod
Speech: I wish to highlight a matter of urgent public concern impacting lakhs of retired employees nationwide, including thousands in my Parliamentary Constituency of Kasaragod. On 4 November 2022, the Hon'ble Supreme Court upheld the right of EPF members to receive pension based on their actual salary. However, the Employees' Provident Fund Organization has failed to implement this judgment in letter and spirit. In fact, EPFO ​​has adopted the pro-rata method to reduce pension, directly contradicting the Ministry of Labour’s own directions and the Supreme Court’s clear mandate of calculating pension on the average of the last 60 months’ salary. Because of this, more than 17.5 lakh pensioners are still waiting for their rightful higher pension after paying huge arrears. In Kasaragod, a large number of Gulf-returned workers and senior citizens are receiving pensions lower than even state welfare pensions, leading to severe hardship. The Himachal Pradesh High Court has already termed EPFO's action as unjustified and bordering on contempt. I urge the Government to immediately withdraw the pro-rata formula, ensure full compliance with the Supreme Court judgment, and release the revised pension and arrears without delay. Re: Need for adherence to Environmental norms in the course of setting up of solar power plants in Barmer, Jaisalmer and Balotra regions in Rajasthan.

Speaker: Ummeda Ram Beniwal — Barmer
Speech: I would like to draw your attention towards the environmental impact being caused by the large scale solar projects being set up in Baroda, Jaisalur and Balotra in Rajasthan. For these projects, important trees like Khejri tree are being cut down indiscriminately, which is destroying the four terrestrial plants. The boundary layer is facing a serious dilemma. At some places, incidents of burning of trees have also come to light, which is an example of environmental pollution. This has resulted in loss of biodiversity in the affected areas, degradation of wildlife habitat, global warming of about 5°C, depletion of groundwater recharge and threats to crop-based livelihoods. The development of renewable energy is important, but it must not come at the cost of damaging the local environment and communities. Therefore, I urge the Honorable Secretary to ensure strict monitoring of solar projects, stop illegal logging, and implement transparent environmental enforcement mechanisms. Also, 25% of the solar project area and tree plantation should be made non-ventilated and projects should be established only on non-cultivable land without using agricultural land, so that the environment can be protected. (Imat) Re: Need for establishment of AIIMS in Peddapalle Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Vamsi Krishna Gaddam — Peddapalle(Sc)
Speech: I rise to draw the attention of the house to the urgent need for establishing an All India Institute of Medical Sciences in Peddapalle Parliamentary Constituency, Telangana. At present, AIIMS Bibinagar is the only AIIMS in the State, and it predominantly serves the Hyderabad region. The people of North Telangana, especially the districts of Peddapalli, Karimnagar, Mancherial, Jagtial and adjoining rural and tribal areas, have to travel long distances for advanced medical treatment, causing delays in care and imposing heavy financial burdens on poor families. Peddapalle is strategically and centrally located within North Telangana, with good rail and road connectivity, making it an ideal hub to serve multiple districts. The region also has adequate availability of suitable land for such a national-level institution. Establishing AIIMS at Peddapalle will ensure timely access to tertiary healthcare for lakhs of people, significantly decongest major hospitals in Hyderabad and strengthen critical specialties in the region.Further, an AIIMS in Peddapalle will act as a catalyst for regional development by generating employment, improving medical education and boosting allied infrastructure. I therefore urge the Government to sanction and establish AIIMS in Peddapalle in the larger interest of equitable healthcare and balanced regional growth in Telangana. (ends) Re: Setting up of a Kendriya Vidyalaya in Ambedkar Nagar, Uttar Pradesh

Speaker: Lalji Verma — Ambedkar Nagar
Speech: In District Ambedkar Nagar North Zone, there is a lack of important education for the children of the working employees of Government Medical College, NTPC, Railway and other departments and the general public, due to which the said working employees face difficulty in getting their children educated. Have to make efforts. To address the above situation, I request the government to establish a central school in Ambedkar Nagar district. (Imat) Re: Need for establishment of modern hospital for beedi workers in Jangipur and Murshidabad in West Bengal along with budgetary allocation for Beedi Workers Welfare Fund.

This is the commemoration of what we call in Manipuri, Nupi Lan, which was a women's resistance against the colonial forces for creating an artificial famine in the State of Manipur in 1939.
