As a result, the significance of the scheme will be lost because, in Kerala, I know that about Rs. 2,500 crore, according to this calculation, the State has to find out, but from where? That means, ultimately, the work will be affected and loss of employment will occur. Therefore, kindly withdraw the Bill. I once again oppose the Bill and request the Government to withdraw the Bill. Thank you. Honorable Speaker: Honorable members, what do you comment on while sitting? I want that there should be comprehensive discussion on every issue in the House. Even after Rule 72, I am giving you a chance to speak. I always try to have a detailed discussion inside the House. You all speak as per the rules and procedures.

Speaker: Shashi Tharoor — Thiruvananthapuram
Speech: Thank you, Mr. Speaker, I would also like to oppose the introduction of the proposed Bill, The Viksit Bharat – G Ram G Bill, which represents a deeply regrettable and retrograde step for our nation and our nation’s commitment to the welfare of its most vulnerable citizens. My first objection, Mr. Speaker, as with others, is the ill-advised decision to remove the name of the father of the nation, for the reasons already given, which I will not repeat. This is not just an administrative tweak; it is an assault on the very spirit and philosophical foundation of this crucial programme. Mahatma Gandhi's vision of Ram Rajya was never a purely political programme. It was a socio-economic blueprint rooted in the empowerment of villages, and his unwavering faith in Gram Swaraj was part of his vision of Ram Rajya. So, the original Act, by bearing his name, acknowledged his profound conviction that true employment guarantee and upliftment must flow from the grassroots, embodying his principle of “the last man first”. To remove the name of Mahatma Gandhi is to strip the Bill of its moral compass and its historical legitimacy, and then to have two languages ​​in the title, merely to make the acronym “G RAM G”, is not just a violation of Article 348, as pointed out in the previous discussion, but it also reminds me of a song, of a lyric from my childhood. We used to sing in childhood – Oh my God, don't do this work, don't defame the name of Ram. We must also seriously question the legislative intent behind the financial restructuring proposed in the Bill. The proposal to impose 40 per cent of the financial burden directly on the State Governments is not just fiscally irresponsible, but it is a measure that threatens to make the entire program unviable. This sudden and massive shift in liability will inevitably make it impossible for poorer States. It will lead to delays in wage payments, reduced days of work, and ultimately the destruction of the Scheme itself. It is a violation of fiscal federalism, which is why I do not think we have the legislative competence to do that. Finally, it also makes the scheme contingent upon executive notification, allowing the Union to decide when and where the scheme will operate. This fundamentally alters the ratio of the programme. Subsidy Affairs Minister; And Minister of Minority Affairs (Shri Vakarne Rivera): Honorable Speaker Sir, your question is big. You gave an opportunity to so many people to speak at the time of the Prophet's introduction. I thank you, you always give everyone a chance to speak. We are timebound. The government has many businesses.… (Interruption) They will run away.… (Interruption) They ask for discussion, then they run away.… (Interruption) This does not happen.… (Interruption) In the interest of the government, we will pass the bills.… (Interruption) But you all take part in the debate.… (Interruption) We have no objection.… (Interruption) We are always ready to listen.… (Interruption) My White House is Limited. When you oppose the introduction of the Bill under Rule 72, the Speaker has repeatedly reminded Spain. We have to oppose the introduction of the Bill only on the ground of the competence of legislation making of this House. It would be fine if you limit this House to the Legislative Assembly only. … (Interruption) Premachandran ji, you are talking about Anchu Chhed 343. Do you know about the Official Gazette Act? We all know that the work of the Union of India is done in two languages, which are Hindi and English. We can use both languages. This practice has been going on since long. You are quoting it unnecessarily under Rule 72. I have no objection, you can say so. I sir want to make this request. … (Interruption) Honorable Speaker: You just make such a request that he listens to you. … (interruption)

Speaker: K. C. Venugopal — Alappuzha
Speech: Sir, thank you very much. Sir, I rise to oppose this Bill under Rule 72. Basically, this is also a fundamental attack on the Constitution. Basically, MNREGA is the right to employment for the common people of this country. That guarantee is therefore the employment. That guarantee now exists only on paper because of this Bill. Sir, already many Members have mentioned that this is a demand driven programme. This destroys the universal coverage. The hollow promises of 125 days is not going to happen at all because of the shift of the financial burden to the States. Forty percent of the fund has to be borne by the States. My point is this. The father of the nation Mahatma Gandhi led the freedom struggle. Hon. Minister Shivraj Chouhan Ji, your name will be remembered as the Minister who removed the father of the nation’s name. Your name will be remembered. … Honorable Speaker: What is your objection? Sir, every day, our hon. Prime Minister is talking about Mahatma Gandhi Ji in a big way. Now, you are bringing a legislation to remove his name. …

Speaker: Dharmendra Yadav — Azamgarh
Speech: Mr. Speaker, on behalf of Honorable Anivesh Yadi ji and the Samajwadi Party, I strongly oppose the amendment bill that is being presented in place of the 'Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act'. There are many reasons for this prohibition. There is a lot of talk in our circle that the Central Government has become so strong economically, it is so supportive. Bill after bill, Act after Act, budget after budget, the financial burden on the profession is continuously being increased. The central government is lightening its economic burden and reducing its responsibilities. … (Legislation) Mr. Speaker, I want to say on behalf of the respected National President and the Samajwadi Party that no person of this class will insult Mahatma Gandhiji. Mayaida Purushottam respects Lord Shri Ram completely. Gandhiji had said his last words 'Hey Ram'. At least do not insult Mahatma Gandhiji, this is our request. Whenever we go anywhere in the world, we offer garlands to the statues of Gandhiji, but inside the body Bapu is being insulted. We socialists will never tolerate this insult. Honorable Minister, you should take back such a statement, otherwise your name will be written in black letter in history. …(Legislation) Honorable Speaker: Sanupraya Suze ji. … (Provision) Honorable Speaker: Sanupraya Suzeji, do you want to speak? … (Interruption) Shrimati Savaj Praya Salje (Baramati): Speaker Sir, I have a humble request to the Government. I can understand. … (interruption) As we move forward year by year and now AI has come, the policy should be changed accordingly. …(Interruption) We do not intend to create change in Poland. From time to time every prophet must be given and thought about. My government's only decision is that you send it to the Standing Committee. There should be a brief discussion on that. Changing the name doesn't matter, work matters. “People will say something, it is people's job to say.” That's why I have a request to the government. There is an objection before that and we protest that you will not remove the name of Mahatma Gandhi from it. We will oppose this with full force. I humbly request them to please discuss this and send whatever changes you are making in this policy decision to the Standing Committee. Government don't waste it. Thank you. Honorable Speaker: Honorable Minister. …(Provision) Honorable Speaker: Everyone has come to their senses. Honorable Minister. … (interruption)

Speaker: Brijmohan Agrawal — Raipur
Speech: In India, there is a restriction on file size while uploading files like Aadhaar card, photo etc. on almost all government portals, without which the file is not uploaded on that portal. For example, the size of the lease/lease agreement file on the GST portal is limited to just one MB (1 MB), without which the GST portal rejects that document. The user has to take the help of unauthorized websites to reduce the file size. Even the Parliament Dartan portal has similar limitations on uploading similar file sizes. These websites and apps are unauthorized, which increases the risk of constructive document data breach and privacy violation. In the era of LLM and AI, this confidential data of ours is being misused. I urge the government to take immediate cognizance of this matter and identify and remove such file size restrictions from such portals being used by all government departments, state governments and private agencies or provide integrated press solutions to the users on those portals only. (Ijat) Re: Need to include algorithmic accountability and platform liability under proposed Digital India Act.

Speaker: Mukesh Dalal — Surat
Speech: AI is next generation technology of the world. India is also rapidly advancing in AI positioning itself as a new player globally. World's largest tech giants are looking towards India to invest billions of dollars into AI & cloud infrastructure in India. India's "India AI Mission" has Rs 10371.92 cr. funding to boost AI ecosystem. India plans to have contribution of $1 trillion in its economy by 2035. AI adoption is surging in defence, telecommunication, electronics, healthcare, agriculture, education & fintech. Since AI is relatively a newer concept in India, it is yet framing AI policies, rules and regulations on data protection, safety, ethics & mitigating deepfakes. Of all these deepfakes are biggest concern & threats to the country, particularly for youth, women, & political leaders & reputed citizens of the country. These AI-generated fake videos/audio can mislead people, damage reputation, & spread distress, disgust & distrust in the country. India's Cyber ​​Crime Coordination Center (I4C) at present tracks deepfake cases. India's IT rules 2021 mandate platforms to remove deepfake cases in India. Yet, I suggest that to safeguard people against misuse of AI, the Government needs to draft still stricter laws under Digital India Act & create awareness among people about AI. I suggest that the Digital India Act may include AI-specific clauses addressing algorithmic accountability & platform liability. Re: Need to check empanelment eligibility of private hospitals under Ayushman Bharat Scheme and lift Inter-State Restriction under Ayushman Bharat Scheme

Speaker: Tokhan Sahu — Bilaspur
Speech: To provide medical facilities to poor people, Ayushman Yojana has been implemented by the Prime Minister, Honorable Shri Narendra Modi, in which eligible people are provided treatment up to Rs 5 lakh in private hospitals. Private hospitals have been included in the scheme but the official hospitals of Nagpur and Maharashtra are either not included in this scheme or they say that Madhya Pradesh's Ayush Mankar Kart does not work here. My constituency (Madhya Pradesh) is adjacent to Nagpur and since the medical system in Nagpur is developed from the east, patients suffering from serious diseases are forced to go to Nagpur for treatment. Despite having Ayushman card, they do not get treatment under the scheme. In such a situation, one has to get treatment by selling the savings and property. My request is that a provision should be made in this scheme that at the time of granting permission to operate the hospital, it will be mandatory for the hospitals having more than a prescribed limit of 10 levels and the hospitals having the facility to treat particularly serious diseases, to get registered for the treatment of patients holding Ayushman card. This misconception should also be removed that patients from Madhya Pradesh will not be treated under Ayushman Yojana in Maharashtra. (Ijat) Re: Need to transfer major Railway Stations presently under the Banaras division to Sonepur division and restoration of sections currently under Muzaffarpur division

Speaker: Rajiv Pratap Rudy — Saran
Speech: At present several key sections of my Saran Parliamentary Constituency such as Chhapra, Masrakh, and Kaptaganj fall under the Banaras Division of the North Eastern Railway (NER), causing significant operational and administrative inconvenience. Simultaneously, the Sonepur Railway Division of the East Central Railway (ECR) is facing a continuous erosion of its jurisdiction and revenue base. Important stations and sections have been repeatedly transferred, and the recent exclusion of the Muzaffarpur–Karpurigram section from Sonepur to the Samastipur Division has further weakened Sonepur’s operational control and financial stability. This fragmentation has disrupted planning, infrastructure development, and passenger services. I therefore propose that major stations presently under the Banaras Division (NER) transferred to the Sonepur Division of the ECR, and that sections shifted to Muzaffarpur Division be restored to Sonepur division. This realignment will enhance administrative convenience, improve operational efficiency, and ensure a fair and balanced distribution of revenue. Re: Need for permanent riverbank embankment in Palamu and Garhwa districts of Jharkhand

Speaker: Vishnu Dayal Ram — Palamau(Sc)
Speech: I want to draw the attention of the government towards the rapid erosion taking place by Koel, Son and Amanat rivers in Garhwa and Palamu district under Palamu sub-division area. From village Mokhapi in Kandi block of Garhwa district, Koel river and Sanu river stretch from Sandu Pipru, Jayanagara, Khardha, Gada, Gada Khadu, Kasanid. From Deepru to Srinagar, villages Narayanpur, Bararih, Bankat, Gadakhdut, Sonparu Wa, Narwaih, Kalagada, Bajlayari, Baju Nayad Javigha, Sadaki, Jarwari, Humrsota and up to Srinagar are facing severe erosion due to the strong flow of Son river. Continuous erosion is going on by Amanat and Koel rivers from village Barkagaon of Mejdininagar in Palamu district to Khanwa, Jor and Jasig Ra. Due to this erosion, irrigated and fertile agricultural land extending for about 6-7 kilometers is being destroyed, and now residential areas at many places are also becoming vulnerable to erosion, due to which rural families are being forced to migrate. It is noteworthy that I have been raising the same issue through the House in the past as well. I request the Ministry of Water Resources, Government of India to coordinate with the Government of Jharkhand and ensure construction of embankments and permanent anti-erosion measures in the above affected area without any delay. (Ijat) Re: Development of tourism, industrial growth and employment opportunities in Kushinagar District, Uttar Pradesh

Speaker: Nishikant Dubey — Godda
Speech: Today I want to draw your attention towards a very important problem of Kuhaninagar district. Kuhaninagar is a historical, religious, Buddhist and important area from tourism point of view. There are immense employment opportunities in the field of tourism. Countless tourists from Buddhist sects come here every year. There is also a lot of potential for industrial development in Kudhinagar, but due to absence of industry, local youth do not get adequate employment opportunities, and they have to go to the metropolis in search of employment. There is immense potential for setting up agro-based industries, food-processing units, MSME sector, tourism-service industries and rural-development centers in Kudhinagar. If the central government arranges special schemes, industrial incentives, investment cooperation and training programs in these areas, then a large number of youth will be able to get employment here and economic progress will get a new impetus. Therefore, it is a humble request through you that all concerned ministries should take necessary action to give priority to tourism development, industrial and employment based development schemes in Kuthinagar. (Ijat) Re: Need to provide 50 percent disability Certification to monocular persons

Speaker: Rahul Lodhi — Damoh
Speech: According to the current guidelines of the government, if a person is completely blind in one eye and goes to get a disability certificate made, then only 30 percent disability certificate is made by the doctor, which does not come in the category of disability. On the other hand, a guideline has also been issued by the government that To avail the benefits of the schemes, the person should have 40 percent or more qualification certificate. In such a situation, the disabled person who is blind in one eye neither gets the benefit of any government scheme (especially the students studying abroad are not even able to get scholarship and salary allowance) nor is he able to get the benefit of reservation. It is requested that a new guideline should be issued to issue a certificate of at least 50 percent disability to the disabled people who are blind in one eye, so that they can get the benefits of government schemes and all types of reservations because the disabled people with one hand and one leg are getting all kinds of benefits. A special campaign should be launched by the Government of India to provide employment opportunities to physically challenged people. (Ijat) Re: Construction of ‘Sardar Circuit’ in Gujarat

Speaker: Parbhubhai Vasava — Bardoli(St)
Speech: I want to draw your attention towards a very important issue of Gujrat. Today, remembering the contribution of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel on the occasion of his 150th birth anniversary, we are inspired by his honor and feel the need to build a Sardar Circuit which connects three important places of Baroli, Statue of Unity, and Nazriya. Baroli is a symbol of the freedom struggle. It was here that Sardar Patel led the Baroli Satyagraha in 1928. The courage and struggle shown by the people of Baroli against excessive taxes inspired the entire community. In accordance with the personality of Sardar Saheb, the Statue of Unity, the tallest statue in the country with a height of 182 meters, has been established by the Prime Minister in Ekta Nagar, which gives inspiration and strength of unity to the entire state and the country. With the development of Sardar Circuit, it will also change the social and economic structure of our region. And at the same time it will reveal the unity, self-reliance and collective wisdom of our nation. Therefore, I request that the development of Sardar Circuit be given priority. I request to start Sardar Tourism Circuit connecting the sacred land of Najarat, the birthplace of Sardar Sahib. (Ijat) Re: Need to accord permission for proposed widening of Kolagarh- Bajawala road crossing through FRI (Forest-Research Institute) campus in Dehradun Mrs. Mala Rajyalakshmi Shah (Natri Garhwal): I want to draw the attention of the government towards the widening of forest research institute (FRI) due to interference in its subsidy area, Kolagarh area of Dehradun. Kaulagarh Bajawala road, which borders Forest Research Institute Dehe Radun and further connects to the National Highway Dehe Radun Patta, is very narrow and the process of widening it has been going on for many years. Apart from Kaulagarh Bajawala, people of many further villages use this route to reach Dehe Radun city. For its widening solution, a proposal was prepared by the Provincial Public Works Department, Dahe Radun, which is proposed to be 30 feet wide till Bajawala (about 1500 meters) through the security wall of Kaulagarh Charkhi Gate Forest Research Institute, but due to the interference of Forest Research Institute, the on-site inspection has not been done yet. I request the Union Forest and Environment Minister to register all efforts for the widening of that road so that the people of the area can get relief from the daily traffic jam in Kaulagarh. (Ijat) Re: Need to expedite approval of the Patmada-Bodam Mega lift irrigation project in Jamshedpur Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Bidyut Baran Mahato — Jamshedpur
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the House towards a very important problem of my constituency Jamshedpur in the Ghat East Jibhum district—Patmada-Bodam Mega Lift Irrigation Project. The PFR of this project has been sent to the Central Water Commission and all the necessary information has been provided on time, yet since last five months till April This is causing unnecessary delay in the implementation of the project. This project will provide irrigation facilities to approximately 16,800 hectares of agricultural land. This entire area is dominated by non-residents, where agriculture is the main basis of livelihood. Irrigation availability will not only increase production capacity but will also provide permanent strength to farmers' income and agriculture-based livelihoods. Apart from Jharkhand, vegetables from Patmada and Bordam region are sent to Ojja and West Bengal, hence better irrigation will be very beneficial for these farmers. Also, no new central irrigation project being started in Jharkhand after the year 2011–12 is also a matter of concern. Therefore, I humbly request the government to complete the PFR appraisal of this project soon and include it in the priority list, so that irrigation can be available to lakhs of farmers in my area throughout the year. (Ijat) Re: Need for development of railway platform at Gyanpur Road Railway Station in Bhadohi Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Vinod Kumar Bind — Bhadohi
Speech: Gyanpur Road Railway Station located in my Lok Sabha constituency Bhadohi, (Uttar Pradesh) is one of the most important stations. This station connects Kari-Prayag and apart from Delhi, Mumbai, a large number of passengers reach this station every day for commuting to other areas. Many important development works are required at the relay station, the most important of which is construction of teen tracks for passengers on platform no. 1 and 2, which will provide relief to passengers during rains and heat. Apart from this, expansion of platform number 1 is necessary. The platform is small, due to which rail passengers face difficulty in boarding and deboarding. (Ijat) Re: Need to grant fast-track project status to the Purna-Akola doubling of rail line project in Maharashtra

Speaker: Anup Dhotre — Akola
Speech: Sir, I rise to request the Hon'ble Railway Minister to address the need for doubling the Purna–Akola railway line, a long-pending section crucial for ensuring seamless movement from the South to the North-West. This pre-Independence railway corridor historically connected the princely state of Rajasthan (including Jaipur) to the Deccan region and continues to hold strategic and economic significance. The doubling of this route is essential to decongest traffic, enhance operational efficiency, and support the increasing passenger and freight movement in the region. Therefore, I urge the Hon’ble Minister to grant fast-track project status to the Purna–Akola track-doubling work at the earliest. Re: Need to set up a modern Meteorological Station at Rajsamand district of Rajasthan Mrs. Manhama Kumari Mewar (Rajsamand): Through you, I want to draw the attention of the government towards the lack of modern meteorological station in Rajsamand, Rajasthan. It has been 34 years since Rajsamand was separated from Udaipur, but even today there is a lack of modern and adequate weather equipment in the district. For years, only the old traditional method has been used for weather related information in the district. Due to lack of accurate weather forecast, it is affecting the farmers the most. Accurate weather forecasting has an impact on sowing and harvesting, crop safety and better crop production. If a young weather station with modern equipment is constructed in Rajsamand district, it will have a positive impact on agriculture and other industries. Therefore, through you I request the government to establish a new meteorological center with state-of-the-art equipment in Rajsamand district at the earliest. (Ijat) Re: Maintenance, operation and payment of electricity bills of tubewell under Jal Jeevan Mission in Dausa Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Harish Meena — Tonk–Sawai Madhopur
Speech: I would like to draw your attention to the serious damage caused by the recent devastating floods in Tak and Sawai Madhopur districts of my constituency. Heavy rains during the July monsoon caused devastation in the entire area, thousands of houses were damaged in these areas, farmers' crops were washed away, water bodies were damaged and electricity and drinking water were disrupted. Basic medical facilities like this came to a standstill. I have personally visited the affected villages and seen first-hand the severity of the suffering and damage. People are still struggling with water, food, water and livelihood crises. Therefore, I request the Central Government to declare Tak and Sawai Madhopur districts as specially disaster affected areas and immediately release a special economic package. This package should not be limited to mere relief distribution but should provide adequate assistance for revival of agricultural sector, reconstruction of damaged roads-school buildings-hospitals and re-establishment of livelihood of rural families. Therefore, I request the government to provide immediate help to the flood affected people of my area. Decisive steps should be taken for the revival of these districts by providing immediate special financial assistance. (Ijat) Re: Need for establishment of medical and nursing colleges in Meghalaya

Speaker: Saleng A. Sangma — Tura(St)
Speech: I would like to bring it to the notice of Hon'ble Minister of Health and Family Welfare regarding shortage of specialist doctors, nurses and other technical staff in the state of Meghalaya. Therefore, if Government could take steps to establish two medical colleges and two more nursing colleges along with technical institution relating to health in the State, the health related problems being faced by the people could be addressed. Re: Need to declare Mahatma Basaveshwara Jayanti as a National event and include his ideals in school curriculum in the country

Speaker: Suresh Shetkar — Zahirabad
Speech: I wish to draw the attention of this esteemed House to the urgent need for national recognition of Mahatma Basaveshwara, a visionary 12th-century philosopher and social reformer. His revolutionary ideals of social justice and gender equality, challenging caste discrimination centuries ago, are foundational to the spirit of our Constitution. Mahatma Basaveshwara established the Anubhava Mantapa, often referred to as the world's first spiritual Parliament—a truly democratic platform open to all sections of society, predating institutions like Magna Carta. His principles of "Kayakaive Kailasa" (Work is Worship) and "Dassoha" (Service and Equal Distribution) remain relevant guiding principles for building a fair society today. While Mahatma Basaveshwara Jayanti is celebrated in several states, his universal message warrants national recognition. I humbly request the Central Government to officially declare Mahatma Basaveshwara Jayanti as a formal National Event across the country. Furthermore, I urge the Ministry of Education to include Mahatma Basavanna’s inspiring biography, his message of equality, social harmony, and democratic values ​​in national school textbooks (NCERT/CBSE) to educate our youth and reaffirm India's proud civilizational truth that "India is the Mother of Democracy." Re: Land acquisition for 84 Kosi Parikrama Road in Ayodhya, Uttar Pradesh

Speaker: Awadhesh Prasad — Faizabad
Speech: 84 In the name of Kosi Parikrama road, in Ayodhya, the city of Lord Shri Ram, the land of farmers is being taken on a large scale by the government without giving proper compensation. Their house is being demolished by using sandblaster. No hearing is taking place anywhere. Thousands of farmers are known. I request the Central Government to investigate the cases of 84 people whose houses were destroyed in the name of Kosi Parikrama and to stop this land acquisition with immediate effect. Farmers of Jamalkipur tehsil and Bikapur are particularly affected. (Respect) Re: Agriculture land acquisition by builders for commercial purposes in Muzaffarpur district, Uttar Pradesh

Speaker: Radha Mohan Singh — Purvi Champaran
Speech: Throughout the state, farmers' land is acquired at the rate of crores and sold at high prices by the government and its ruling people. Information about government schemes is leaked to the people in power, due to which these people buy land from farmers at the rate of crores, make residential plots out of it and sell it commercially. In my subsidy area, the fertile land of village Shernagar, Jawalaspur and other villages of Majhu Farnagar will be acquired in the name of residential colony and later it will be sold by the administrators at expensive prices. There is great anger among the farmers due to this. If a farmer loses his land, he loses everything and nothing is left. Even before this, the farmers of the same village were given the fear of housing development and their land was bought at half the price and it was sold by Jabalhar at a higher price of Rs 1 lakh per yard. The layout plan of the proposed Ganga Expressway was leaked by Sasicino and land was purchased there and now it is being sold at expensive prices. My request is that this should be investigated and immediate action should be taken and farmers' land should not be purchased. (Ijat) Re: Need for direct flight connectivity between Kolkata and European countries

Speaker: Yusuf Pathan — Baharampur
Speech: West Bengal is one of the most favored tourist destinations in the country with the state witnessing the 2nd highest foreign tourist footfall in India in 2025. Despite this huge foreign interest in the state, there are no direct flights connecting Kolkata to Europe. This deprives Bengal of both revenue and economic opportunities. Similarly, with over 2.5 lakh passengers traveling from Kolkata to Europe each year, the lack of direct flights imposes heavy costs in terms of time, money and convenience on students, businesspeople and tourists. Multiple MPs have, in the past, petitioned the Ministry of Civil Aviation for improving the connectivity between Kolkata and Europe. In December 2024, the West Bengal State Assembly passed a unanimous resolution seeking the Union Government's intervention to ensure direct flights between Kolkata and Europe. West Bengal’s strategic location will also ensure that direct flight connectivity from Kolkata to Europe will cater not just to the whole Eastern and Northeastern India but also to neighboring countries like Bhutan, Nepal and Bangladesh. Hence, I urge the government to take speedy action to ensure that direct flight connectivity between Kolkata and Europe is established at the earliest. Re: Need to provide financial assistance to set up a dedicated cancer hospital or District Cancer Center in Ranipet district of Tamil Nadu

Speaker: S. Jagathrakshakan — Arakkonam
Speech: I rise to draw the urgent attention of this House to the alarming increase in cancer cases in Ranipet district of my Parliamentary Constituency of Arakkonam, Tamil Nadu. Tamil Nadu Government conducted the screening camps in Ranipet which examined more than 9,500 people, and hundreds of cancer cases—including detection of early-stage breast cancer. These numbers reflect a serious and growing public health challenge. However, Ranipet district does not have a dedicated cancer hospital or district cancer centre. Patients—many of them daily-wage earners—are forced to travel long distances to Chennai or Vellore for diagnosis, chemotherapy and radiotherapy. This delays treatment, increases out-of-pocket expenses, and causes immense hardship for families already struggling with the emotional and financial burden of cancer. I, therefore, request the Honorable Minister of Health and Family Welfare to sanction a dedicated Cancer Hospital or District Cancer Center in Ranipet under central assistance, and to link it with the National Oncology Grid for technical support. This will bring timely, life-saving care within the reach of thousands of people. I urge the Ministry to take this matter on priority and order an immediate assessment of feasibility. Re: Need to set up an additional Kendriya Vidyalaya in Kurnool district, Andhra Pradesh

Speaker: Bastipati Nagaraju Panchalingala — Kurnool
Speech: Respected Speaker, At present, 37 Kendriya Vidyalaya's (KVs) are functioning in the State of Andhra Pradesh and Kurnool district has only one KV. During the academic year 2024–25, enrollment in the lone KV increased by nearly 12%, reaching 809 students, indicating growing demand and pressure on existing infrastructure. Kendriya Vidyalayas play a crucial role in providing quality education, particularly to the children of Central Government employees. With a district population of 22.71 lakh, the current number of KVs in Kurnool is clearly inadequate to meet educational needs. Further, although the Central Government has approved 85 new KVs nationwide, including 8 in Andhra Pradesh, none has been sanctioned for Kurnool district. In view of the rising enrolment, population size, and the strategic importance of equitable access to Central Government schools, I request the Government to sanction additional Kendriya Vidyalaya's for Kurnool district at the earliest. Re: Need to make Kaivalyadhama Health and Yoga Research Center as a National Center of Excellence

Speaker: Shrirang Barne — Maval
Speech: Kai Valyadham, located in my constituency Maval, is a spiritual, medical and research center established with a specific objective to integrate the ancient yogic art and tradition with modern science. Kai Valyadham has been a voluntary commitment to the scientific yogic tradition of Lonavala and its national contribution and this Kai Valyadham was established in the year Yogachayat was organized by Swami Kuvalayanad ji in 1924. This institute is the first institute in India to present Yoga scientifically, which made Yoga science a subject of serious research through research papers like 'Yoga Mimasa'. In 1951, the institute started its first Yoga College and in 1961, it started its Yoga Hospital. Its historical contribution was also recognized in the debate of the Legislative Assembly in the year 1948. Today, when India is moving forward in giving global recognition to Yoga, it is necessary to provide special support to such institutions by the Central Government. I urge the government to create an institution like Kaivalidham as a national center of excellence in yoga, so that India's cultural and scientific heritage gets respect on the global stage. (Ijat) Re: Need to sanction a special package for flood control measures in Khagaria Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Rajesh Verma — Khagaria

Speaker: Mathukumilli Bharat — Visakhapatnam
Speech: Thank you, Sir. I rise to stand in support of the Repealing and Amending Bill, 2025, which addresses a subject that lies at the very heart of effective governance and is also very close to my heart. This is not optional; this is essential for a modern and dynamic democracy. Some of my colleagues in the Opposition who have spoken here have raised certain points, which I will address later in my speech. But one thing on which I believe we can all agree, is that change is the only constant. Our Constitution-makers recognised this timeless truth. They understood that governance must evolve with society and, therefore, built Article 368 as the constitutional engine for reform. They did not give us a static document; they gave us a living Constitution – a framework that entrusts future generations with the responsibility to amend, repeal, and refine laws as India progresses. Over the decades, however, our legislative system has developed serious structural imbalances. I have personally experienced this in many aspects of my life, where governments are very good at adding laws, adding rules, and adding conditions, but are very poor at removing or updating outdated ones. This unnecessary burden is faced by everyone – from the common man to the poorest of the poor, and even by the highly wealthy. Every new policy brings with it fresh compliance requirements, new penalties, and additional layers of regulation. Rarely do we pause to ask whether old provisions have outlived their relevance or whether they now contradict newer laws. The result is regulatory congestion, which burdens citizens, businesses, and Government officers alike, creating confusion and discretionary enforcement. We have seen how some bad actors misuse this discretion to harm the interests of citizens and even the nation. I am happy to note that the current NDA Government has taken concrete steps over the last decade to relieve citizens and businesses of this burden. More than 40,000 unnecessary compliances have been abolished, easing procedural hurdles and reducing red tape. Over 1,500 outdated and redundant laws have been repealed, clearing the statute book of colonial-era provisions and laws that no longer serve India’s development. I would like to point out a few things here. Some Members of the Opposition have said that we are changing laws within three years or four years. We must recognise that we are living in the era of artificial intelligence and quantum computing, where the world is changing rapidly, and we have to keep adapting. One must also reflect on the approach we adopt: whether we wait endlessly for perfection or ensure that we are good enough to move forward, while continuing to amend and improve as we go along. If I had to choose between perfection and being good enough to move forward, I would always choose being good enough because we should not be trapped in policy paralysis. This is important to highlight because during the UPA regime between 2004 and 2014, only a single repeal Bill was introduced, which repealed just two outdated laws. This starkly contrasts the approach of the current NDA Government with that of the earlier UPA regime, which largely neglected the task of revising and repealing obsolete laws. Sir, I am an active Member of the Select Committee on the Jan Vishwas (Amendment of Provisions) Bill,2025 and I have been participating actively in rationalising penal provisions and simplifying compliance, with the objective of improving ease of living and ease of doing business. I hope that the same principle keeps continuing. I am happy to hear that our hon. Minister Shri Piyush Goyal has mentioned that Jan Vishwas 3.0 is already under way. I think this is the sign of a healthy democracy where we keep evolving and keep looking at our past. While these reforms at the Centre have eased compliance and built trust, we need to do so at the State also where we have a lot of interaction with citizens. In our State, some regrettably damaging laws like the Land Titling Act or the Three Capitals Act that the earlier Government tried to push through, have caused a lot of pain and confusion in citizens’ minds, damaging trust. As soon as the NDA Government came to power in Andhra Pradesh, we provided clarity and have repealed these laws which have gone against the interests of citizens. While these were in particular nefarious things, the overarching principle that repealing or revising the problematic laws that are creating conflict or laws that are outdated, is essential for bringing back transparency, trust, and predictability in governance. I look forward to aiding our State Government in continuing to do so. The Repealing and Amending Bill, 2025 is not merely an exercise in removing obsolete statutes; it reaffirms our fundamental principle of good governance that laws which have outlived their purpose must be repealed with the same resolve with which progressive laws are enacted. This House bears the responsibility not only to legislate for the present, but to free future generations from needless regulatory bodies. Hon. Chairperson, I thank you for giving me this opportunity to speak on this important Bill and I wish that the Government continues to do this to ensure that there is maximum governance, minimum government, empowering our citizens for a trust-based and efficient society. 1447 hours

Speech: Thank you, Madam, for giving me an opportunity to speak on the Repealing and Amending Bill, 2025, which seeks to repeal outdated enactments and correct formal defects in existing laws. It protects accrued rights, liabilities, past actions, and pending proceedings, thereby ensuring legal continuity and constitutional certainty under Articles 21 and 300A. Under the Second Schedule – General Clauses Act, 1897, Section 27, replacing “registered post” with “speed post with registration” aligns statutory language with modern postal systems and reduces procedural delays. Similarly, amendments to the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, modernizing service of summons through proof-of-delivery mechanisms, will improve dispute resolution timelines – an essential factor for ease of doing business, trade certainty, and cross-border commerce. While the intent of the Bill is positive, certain concerns merit attention. First, Clause 2 provides for a blanket repeal of multiple Amendment Acts without a consolidated restatement, which may create interpretational challenges for courts, lawyers, and administrators. Second, under the Second Schedule, procedural amendments are introduced without clear transition guidelines or capacity-building measures for subordinate courts and district administrations. Without such support, the benefits of these reforms may not fully reach the ground. I would like to seek some clarifications from the Government. Speaker: Manickam Tagore — Virudhunagar
Speech: Thank you, Speaker, Sir. This Bill was introduced at 12:30 pm today, and at 4:30 pm we are having a discussion on it. The main question is, why is the Government rushing too far for this law to be made? This House has debated many Bills, some technical, some transactional, and some routine Bills. But, today's Bill is none of those. This Bill asks for fundamental questions about our Republic. Is insurance a public promise or merely a private product? Is protection a right of citizenship or a privilege of purchasing power? Insurance is not just about policies and premiums; it is about who bears risk in our society. Speaker, Sir, when risk is pushed away from the State and on to citizens, democracy itself becomes fragile. Let us remember where we came from. In 1956, India had nationalized life insurance, not because markets failed, but because markets excluded. Indira Gandhi said: “The purpose of nationalization is not profit but protection.” LIC was born not as a corporation but as a constitutional instrument of social security. It insured farmers with uncertain incomes, workers without formal contracts, and families whose only asset was hope. For decades, LIC did what private capital could not. It went to villages, took long-term risks, and invested in national development. That is the Congress's idea of ​​insurance. We believe in inclusion, not extraction, and we believe in solidarity, not speculation. What does this Bill really do? This bill is wrapped in comforting words, sabka beema, sabki raksha. But, let us read the fine print. Section 3(a)(a) allows 100 percent FDI in insurance. It is not 74 per cent; it is not shared control; but complete foreign ownership. That means, pricing decisions made outside India, investment priorities set by global boards, profits repatriated abroad, and all in a sector that controls household savings, long-term capital, and social risk pooling. This is not reform, Sir. This is the withdrawal of the State. The Government claims that this is the necessity for capital. But, Sir, facts matter. Many private insurers have not even used the existing 74 per cent FDI limit. If capital was the real problem, why was the 74 percent not enough? The truth is uncomfortable. This decision is not driven by necessity, but, it is driven by ideology. An ideology that believes that the State must retreat, the market will magically include, and citizens must feed for themselves. The Congress Party rejects this ideology, clearly and unapologetically. What happens when insurance is fully privatized, Sir? We do not need theory. We have experience. Look at the Prime Minister's Fasal Bima Yojana. Farmers pay rising premiums; the claims are delayed or disputed; and the insurers remain protected. Look at Ayushman Bharat. Private hospitals deny high-cost procedures; cashless care becomes conditional; and States report unpaid bills running into lakhs of crores. When profit enters without accountability, risk quietly exits the insurer's balance sheet and enters the citizen's life. That is what happens when insurance forgets its social role. Speaker, Sir, this Bill does not just open the door to foreign capital, it tilts the playing field itself. Private and foreign insurers will cherry-pick low-risk urban customers, exit rural and informal segments, and re-insure profits abroad. But, who will remain? It is LIC, and public insurers as insurers of last resort. This is not competition, but a systematic weakening. You privatize profits, and socialize losses. Congress will not accept this silent dismantling of India's largest and strongest public institution. Insurance is not a shop, but it is a financial backbone. Premiums are not spare change, but they are the long-term national savings. With 100 per cent FDI, dividends will flow out, re-insurance payments will flow out, and decision-making will also flow out. At a time when India needs domestic capital for infrastructure, climate resilience and social protection, this Bill allows an outflow of patient capital. Sir, that is not nation-building. This is financial dependency. Hon. Speaker, Sir, we are not standing here to defend stagnation. Congress believes in reforms. But, it should be reforms with responsibility. Our alternative is clear. Strengthen LIC and public insurers, expand coverage through public-private balance, not surrender, use regulation to enforce inclusion and treat insurance as a social infrastructure. We believe growth must be shared, not siphoned. Sir, let me end where I began. The Bill asks a question. Will India say protection is a right or protection is a product? Will we say the State stands with citizens in uncertainty or citizens must negotiate risk alone? Speaker, Sir, Congress chooses the Republic over the market, citizens over corporations and protection over profit. That is why we oppose this Bill, not in anger but in conviction. 1646 hours

Speaker: Ganti Harish Madhur — Amalapuram(Sc)
Speech: Thank you, Sir, for allowing me the opportunity to speak on the Sabka Bima Sabki Raksha Bill, 2025. This Bill is a timely and necessary step to modernise India’s insurance framework. Allowing up to 100 per cent Foreign Direct Investment will help expand the sector and improve consumer choice, while the strengthened role of the IRDAI will enhance regulatory oversight and protect the policyholders. The creation of Policyholders’s Education and Protection Fund reinforces these reforms by recognising that insurance can expand meaningfully only when awareness and trust are built among the consumers. Sir, one provision that particularly deserves appreciation is the removal of the mandatory minimum paid-up capital requirement for the insurance cooperative societies. This will encourage community-based and region-specific insurance models, which are crucial for expanding coverage in under-served areas. However, I would like to highlight certain concerns where the intent of the Bill can be further strengthened. Firstly, insurance penetration in India’s rural areas remains deeply inadequate. India’s insurance penetration is only about 3.7 per cent, well below the global average of seven per cent. While easing capital norms for cooperative insurers is a step in the right direction, the reality is that India currently has only very few cooperative insurers. Therefore, micro-insurance assumes critical importance for low-income households, which provides protection against health emergencies, crop loss, natural disasters and loss of earning members. To scale micro-insurance meaningfully, it is essential to encourage smaller and niche players who understand local risks and regional needs. Therefore, rather than a rigid capital threshold, empowering IRDAI to prescribe proportionate and risk-based capital norms would allow flexibility while maintaining prudential safeguards. Secondly, though opening the sector to 100 per cent FDI will increase competition and consumer choice, it is equally important to ensure that there is a level-playing field between the public sector and the private insurers. At present, a TDS of two per cent applies only to public sector insurance companies while the private insurers are exempt. This regulatory asymmetry places public sector insurers at a competitive disadvantage. Therefore, to realise the objective of the Bill, such unequal tax treatment must be addressed. As more insurers enter the market, the absence of composite licensing remains a structural limitation. At present, insurers are required to operate separate entities for life insurance and general and health insurance, which increases compliance costs and prevents the offering of integrated products. Allowing composite licensing would, therefore, enable insurers to offer bundled solutions covering life and health insurance and savings under one roof, improving efficiency and delivering greater value to consumers. The gaps in the insurance coverage becomes most visible when disaster strikes and livelihoods are wiped out overnight. India faces severe and recurring disaster risks due to its geography and demography. In my home State of Andhra Pradesh, particularly in coastal districts such as Dr. B. R. Ambedkar Konaseema, communities face floods and cyclones almost every year. Despite this repeated exposure, disaster insurance coverage in India remains weak, particularly for economically vulnerable households and infrastructure in high-risk areas with more than 90 per cent natural disasters being uninsured against. The Finance Committee recommended parametric insurance as a solution offering faster payouts and clear triggers. I am happy to note that the NDMA has constituted a working group to develop single-peril parametric insurance products for India. Additionally, I would like to suggest to the hon. Minister using provisions in the legislation to speed up implementation of parametric insurance and also opening up use of government technology such as Weather Information Network and Data System (WINDS) for the same. Similar gaps, in insurance protection exists for migrant workers working abroad. The Indian Government offers the Pravasi Bharatiya Bima Yojana as a mandatory insurance scheme for all Indian workers holding ECR-category passports and going to ECR countries. However, the Committee on External Affairs has noted that since the inception of the scheme, only about 2,197 claims have been settled out of 80 lakh policies, and only 156 of 418 claims were settled. This low settlement ratio indicates deeply concerning systemic shortcomings. Coming from a State with a high number of migrant workers to other countries, especially the Gulf region, I would like to recommend that the Ministry of Finance work with the Indian Missions in claim facilitation, stricter oversight of insurers, and timely reimbursement to protect vulnerable migrant workers. As I conclude, I would like to take a moment to commend the Delhi Police for cracking an insurance scam caller earlier today involving a fake call centre that preyed on individuals whose policies were nearing maturity. While we celebrate their work, we must be equally vigilant of dangers posed by scamsters and fraudsters in the insurance sector. We must ensure that all steps have been taken to provide the government with adequate support for modern technology so that they may protect our people from such frauds. With this, I conclude my speech and on behalf of my Party I support the Bill. Thank you. 1725 hours

Again, in 2021, there was a significant increase in the private sector's share of premium, and it was enhanced to 74 per cent by removing the Indian ownership and control requirement. Now, there is a move to increase FDI to 100 per cent in the insurance sector. I would like to draw your attention to the fact that there are many demerits of 100 per cent FDI in the insurance sector. These sections lay down a statutory ceiling on shareholders, transfer of shares, ownership thresholds and voting rights. Speaker: Rajmohan Unnithan — Kasaragod
Speech: Thank you very much, Chairman, Sir. The name of this Bill promises protection for all but in reality it is a blueprint for surrendering India's insurance sector to foreign corporate interests. On the one hand the Government lectures the nation on self-reliance, and on the other hand it allows 100 per cent foreign ownership in a sector that safeguards the life savings of crores of Indians. If this is Aatmanirbhar Bharat, why does the Government believe that foreign capital must fully own and control the Indian insurance companies? Why is Indian capital suddenly inadequate? Even before liberalization in 2000, LIC agents worked tirelessly to deliver exceptional results, contributing to India's financial growth and infrastructural development. Companies may compete by offering higher incentives, eroding agent loyalty, and shifting focus from policyholder welfare to self-interest. LIC agents currently manage more than 25 products. Open architecture will require knowledge of hundreds of products, resulting in superficial advice and suboptimal customer service. Exclusivity fosters healthy competition. Allowing agents to move freely across companies will dilute innovation and lead to stagnation in the sector. There will be compromise in post-sales service. Insurance is not just selling policies, it is about continuous support. Agents shifting between companies cannot provide consistent after-sales services, harming policyholders. Development Officers are the backbone of LIC. They recruit, train and mentor agents. Their performance is tied to the business generated by their agents, with accountability including possible termination in case of significant decline in business. Allowing agents to work for competing insurers will jeopardize their livelihoods. Hon. Members, the Bill threatens the livelihood of agents and Development Officers, LIC's legacy and operational model, ethical standards in insurance, financial stability, and inclusion of millions of policyholders. Insurance should protect citizens and strengthen national resilience. This Bill does the opposite. Therefore, I categorically reject the Bill and demand that it be withdrawn or referred to a Standing Committee for thorough scrutiny. For these reasons, I strongly and unequivocally oppose the Sabka Bima Sabki Raksha (Amendment of Insurance Laws) Bill, 2025. 1940 hrs

Speaker: Jyotiraditya Scindia — Guna
Speech: Sir, I would like, through you, to respond to my good friend Maran sahib on both his very pertinent questions. The first question has to do with satellite connectivity. Worldwide, we have seen that areas that are unconnectable through normal BTS, backhaul, or broadband connectivity through OFC, the only route to be able to connect those citizens across the world is through SATCOM. Therefore, India has taken that step of ensuring that SATCOM is also available to every customer across the length and breadth of our country. As Telecom Minister, my goal is to ensure that I am able to offer the full bouquet of services to every customer, and then the customer makes his or her choice as to which service they would like to avail of, and at which price point. For SATCOM, as you are very well aware, the policy is very well in place. The spectrum is going to be assigned administratively. We have already given out three licenses – one to Starlink, another to OneWeb, and the third to Reliance. There are two aspects that need to be looked at prior to them starting services. One, of course, is the whole issue of spectrum assignment. Within that, there are two issues. One is the administrative cost of that spectrum, which will be decided by the regulatory authority, in this case TRAI, which is currently working on defining the pricing. The second is the issue of security-related clearances from the enforcement agencies for these players. In order to assist them in clearing those security requirements, we have given them sample spectrum to be able to demonstrate. All three players are working on this today. As soon as they are able to demonstrate that they have complied with the security norms, such as hosting the international gateway within India and so on and so forth, approval will be given to them to be able to extend that service to customers. Another question he raised was with regard to pricing. Pricing is not something that the Government can dictate; it is something that firms can dictate. India has had a very strong track record of showing the world how high volumes and low pricing can lead not only to great penetration, but also great amount of revenue for firms. This has been demonstrated both in the mobile space as well as the broadband space. Today, as far as telecom is concerned, we have the highest number of subscribers at the lowest rates possible. What used to be 50 paise a minute in 2014 is today 0.03 paise a minute – a 97 per cent decline in cost. Similarly, for data, one GB, which earlier used to cost Rs. 287, today costs Rs. 9 per GB, which is roughly about nine cents, whereas the world average is USD 2.49. So, we are at five percent of the world's cost today in India. I am sure that, in the time to come, SATCOM players will also realize the concept of economies of scale and will possibly try and price their services so that they can get the maximum penetration. However, that is not something that the Government can dictate, and I want to be very clear on that. His second question was with regard to in-flight connectivity. That is very much on our table and is in process. The rules are being framed, as far as the Department of Telecom is concerned. We have already identified the framework. Now, there are two processes. Having been the Minister of Civil Aviation in the last Government, I would like to offer this to Mr. Maran as well.
