Speaker: Shripad Naik — North Goa
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the House towards the seriously affected health system of my constituency North-East Delhi. Due to the huge influx of patients every day and the local population of about 50 people, they are facing severe chaos due to limited resources and lack of doctors. Many patients suffering from serious diseases are dying due to lack of timely treatment, which is extremely dangerous and painful. Therefore, I urge the Honorable Health Minister to- 1. A decision should be taken to immediately strengthen the current health system. 2. A Men's College should be established in North-East Delhi. 3. A state-of-the-art hospital with at least 500 beds with modern machines and testing equipment should be constructed soon. With these necessary steps, the vulnerable population of the area will be able to get access to accessible, effective and quality health services. (Inat) Re: Need to set up a Zoological Park on vacant forest land in Farrukhabad Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Mukesh Rajput — Farrukhabad
Speech: I would like to request the Honorable Forest and Environment Minister of Uttarakhand that in my subsidy area Ruyabad, about 160 hectares (2000 bighas) of unused land of the Forest Department is lying at a distance of just two kilometers from Ruyyabad city. Very close to this land, the Ruiabad Expressway connecting the Ganga Expressway to the Banda Expressway is also passing. Ram Nagariya Fair is also organized on the banks of Ganga in Suryaabad city itself. In this district, there are many important places of national and sub-national fame like Neem Karori Dham, Shri Rampur, the national Buddhist pilgrimage site, Sun Kasa, Napi, the capital of Jainism and Mahabharata period Drapur is also situated here. If there is unused land of the forest department near the city of Ruyabad, on which there is no plantation. If a National Zoological Park or Aquarium is built on such land, then 160 hectares of land will not only be rejuvenated but also two thousand bighas of land of this forest department can be saved from encroachment. (Inat) Re: Need to develop Talgaria Railway Station in Jharkhand as a satellite railway station and ensure stoppage of trains running between Delhi and Odisha at the station.

Speaker: Sambit Patra — Puri
Speech: Prayagraj has undoubtedly been the educational capital of North Prussia. The institutions established here have made an incomparable contribution to nation building. Allahabad University is called the 'Oxroad of the East'. Established in 1887, this university, the fourth oldest in the world, has provided leadership in every field, from the freedom struggle to the making of modern India. The institutions here have proved that Prayagraj has a strong ecosystem of education and excellence. There is such a fertile ground of knowledge here where any new initiative of quality education can quickly take root. Despite such a rich educational history, today Prayagraj lacks a top-class management institute. Today, as India moves towards the goal of a self-reliant India, we need skilled managers, entrepreneurs and business leaders who can drive the economic engine of the New India. Institutions like IIM are key to meeting this need. Establishing an IIM in Peru will not only create an institute, it will remove the regional bias, it will prevent students from entire North Pradesh and surrounding areas from migrating for quality management education. (Inat) Re: Need to undertake a comprehensive feasibility study for establishment of Small Modular Nuclear Reactors (SMRs) in Bhanjanagar or Boudh district, Odisha

Speaker: Sukanta Kumar Panigrahi — Kandhamal
Speech: I wish to raise an important matter concerning India's clean energy transition and regional development. India’s electricity demand is rising rapidly, and meeting our national targets of 500 GW non-fossil energy capacity by 2030 and Net-Zero emissions by 2070 requires reliable and continuous clean power sources. In this context, Small Modular Nuclear Reactors (SMRs) offer a promising solution. SMRs are being adopted globally due to their inherent safety features, modular scalability, lower land and water requirements, faster deployment timelines, and reduced overall project complexity compared to conventional nuclear plants. Establishing an SMR in Bhanjanagar or Boudh district would strengthen Odisha’s energy security and contribute to balanced industrial growth in eastern India. The proposed site, located near existing nuclear-related infrastructure at Chhatrapur, provides advantages of logistics, workforce availability, and connectivity to upcoming industrial corridors. This initiative would accelerate socio-economic progress in tribal and rural belts through sustainable employment, skill development, and investment inflow while supporting India’s leadership in clean energy technologies. I, therefore, request the Hon’ble Minister for Power and Ministry of New & Renewable Energy to kindly direct DAE, NPCIL and concerned agencies to undertake a comprehensive feasibility study at the earliest. (ends) Re: Need to introduce a scheme for connecting Agricultural fields with main road networks

Speaker: Darshan Singh Choudhary — Hoshangabad
Speech: Indian economy is based on agriculture, but for a long time agriculture and gram did not get the required priority. Pradhan Mantri Gramin Sadak Yojana proved to be an important achievement in rural development, as a result of which 7,83,620 km of roads were constructed and 1,56,399 habitations were connected and crores of rural citizens got direct benefits. This is evidence that road connectivity is the basic foundation of rural development. There is now a need to ensure strengthened access to land to link agricultural production with the modern economic structure. Almost every family in India is connected to agriculture, hence road connectivity to the fields is not just a convenience but is essential to increase production, transportation, storage and marketing capacity. Therefore, I have a humble request to the Central Government to start “Sweet Sadak Yojana” to connect the fields of every farmer of the state with the main road. This should be started from the agriculture-oriented state of Madhya Pradesh, especially the agriculture-centred Hoshangabad Lok Sabha, which will reduce transportation costs, reach the market on time, preserve the quality of agricultural produce and give a new impetus to the rural economy. This will bring real change in the lives of the first crore farmers and will be a decisive step towards India becoming self-reliant with a strong agricultural infrastructure. (Inat) Re: Need to link Darbhanga with Patna-Purnia expressway

Speaker: Ummeda Ram Beniwal — Barmer
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the House to the serious decline in MNREGA wage payments in the remote and extremely remote areas of Rajasthan. The number of active workers has declined from 14.3 crore in 2023–24 to 13.2 crore in 2024–25, with a large number being in rural areas. The family is not able to get guaranteed employment. This situation has adversely affected the income, nutritional security and livelihood of workers in the desert region of Western Rajasthan. Many workers have been waiting for months to be paid due to unpaid wages for 2024–25, and new and old workers have been left out of work due to unnecessary delays in job card approvals in many districts. Delay in payment even after complete work execution is increasing rural indebtedness. Therefore, I request the Rural Development Minister to ensure timely payment of back wages, serialization of job card system, special review of social audit and state-wise data of the due amount should be presented in the House. Apart from this, I also request that the people engaged in MNREGA program from time to time should The outstanding payment of workers should be released immediately so that they can get relief. (Inat) Re: Need to expedite completion of Phase-II work on Mallavaram-Bhopal- Bhilwara-Vijaipur Natural Gas Pipeline Project

Speaker: Vamsi Krishna Gaddam — Peddapalle(Sc)
Speech: I rise to draw the urgent attention of the Government to the delay in completing Phase-II of the Mallavaram–Bhopal–Bhilwara–Vijaipur (MBBVPL) Natural Gas Pipeline, which passes through Manthani, Ramagundam, Mancherial, and Bellampally in Peddapalli district. Phase-I of the project has already been completed, and gas supply to RFCL Ramagundam has commenced. However, Phase-II, which includes the spur lines and tap-off points on the Mancherial–Bellampally–Nagpur stretch, remains pending for a long time. This delay is denying our region access to natural gas for industries, the CNG/PNG network, transport needs, and MSME expansion. The availability of natural gas is essential for reducing energy costs, improving industrial competitiveness, and attracting new investments to the district. Completion of Phase-II would also enable the development of a dedicated Gas-Based Industrial Cluster in Peddapalli, creating jobs and supporting the economic aspirations of the local population. I therefore urge the Government to take immediate steps to complete the remaining portion of the pipeline, establish the required tap-off points in our district, and ensure timely gas availability for industrial and public use. This project has the potential to significantly boost employment and industrial growth in Peddapalli, and its early completion is essential. Re: Need to review Survey Nos. 881 and 882 which classified the land of Chaukka Desam, Kodassry village and Chalakudy Taluk in Kerala as Reserve Forest.

Speaker: Benny Behanan — Chalakudy
Speech: I wish to highlight the long-pending request for pattayam (land title deeds) for over one hundred families in Chaukka Desam, Kodassery Village, Chalakudy Taluk. Nearly sixty families have lived here for more than a century, but pattayam distribution remains stalled because Survey Nos. 881 and 882 continue to be classified as Reserve Forest in Central records. These lands do not share boundaries with any forest area, and several pattayams were issued here between 1970 and 1980. Multiple official surveys—including the 1992 joint survey, subsequent joint and digital surveys, and the 2024 data collection by the Kerala Revenue Department—have clearly confirmed long-standing occupation. Due to the existing classification, residents are denied housing benefits, face building restrictions, and cannot conduct land transactions. As the State can proceed only with Central clearance, I request the Ministry to review this classification and grant approval for pattayam distribution. Re: Non-implementation of Post-Matric Scholarship for SC Students in Bihar

Speaker: Manoj Kumar — Sasaram(Sc)
Speech: According to PMS SC Nishaanshe 2021, the state government has to give freeship card to every eligible enrolled student on the basis of which the student will get enrolled in the educational institution without payment of any fee and later the central and state government will jointly pay the fee. Under the said scheme, in the last three years 2021-22, 2022-23 and 2023- 24, Uttar Pradesh got ₹ 2132.11 crore, Maharashtra got ₹ 2075.41 crore, 966466 beneficiaries, Tamil Nadu got ₹ Earned Rs 1721.72 crore, beneficiary is 1628682. In the above three years, the Central Government has allocated Rs. 11794 crores on this scheme, out of which Bihar got only Rs. 58.83 crores because the state government here is not implementing the scheme as per the anti-addiction guidelines of the Central Government. Therefore, I request the Central Government through the House to implement the Freeship Cards in Bihar under the PMS-SC Scheme 2021 of the Government of India from the date of implementation by the Central Government i.e. from the financial year 2020-21 and the full tuition fees and other mandatory allowances of the scheduled students of the state should be reimbursed. The fee should be reimbursed through scholarship. (Inat) Re: Need for establishment of ‘Mappila Kala Kendram – Arabic and Urdu Literature Center of Excellence’ at Charummoodu in Alappuzha district, Kerala

Speaker: Kodikkunnil Suresh — Mavelikara(Sc)
Speech: I wish to draw the attention of the Hon’ble Minister of Culture and Minister of Education to the urgent need for establishing “Mappila Kala Kendram – Arabic and Urdu Literature Center of Excellence” at Charummoodu, Mavelikkara, Kerala. The Mappila community has made remarkable contributions to India’s composite culture through its unique traditions of Arabi-Malayalam and Urdu literature, music, and spiritual expressions. Despite this rich legacy, there is a significant gap in institutional mechanisms to preserve, study, and promote these art forms, manuscripts, and oral traditions. Charummoodu, situated at the cultural intersection of Alappuzha and Kollam districts, has historically served as a nurturing center for Mappilappattu, Arabi-Malayalam literature, qasida recitations, and other traditional art forms. Establishing a Center of Excellence would enable systematic research, documentation, training, translation, publication, and cultural exchange. It would also help conserve rare manuscripts and oral histories that are currently at risk of being lost. The proposed Kendram may be developed under the Ministry of Culture or in partnership with institutions such as the Kerala Folklore Academy, Sahitya Akademi, or the National Council for Promotion of Urdu Language. I urge the Government to consider this initiative at the earliest to safeguard and promote India’s diverse literary and cultural heritage. Re: Need to strengthen healthcare facilities in Basti Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Krishna Prasad Tenneti — Bapatla(Sc)
Speech: In my constituency Basti, North Prussia, medical and hospital facilities are very low in proportion to the population. Even after the establishment of a medical college, proper diagnosis of diseases is not possible due to lack of doctors and testing equipment. As a result, patients are immediately transferred to other medical colleges, the financial burden of which poor people are unable to bear. Due to lack of health facilities and health care providers, many area residents remain deprived of necessary treatment. Also, due to the wide availability of counterfeit medicines, people become victims of more serious diseases instead of getting healthy. Therefore, you are requested to make efforts to provide doctors and modern testing equipment to strengthen the health facilities in our subsidized area of ​​Basti, Uttar Pradesh. (Inat) Re: Need to increase and operate Paddy Procurement Centers in Uttar Pradesh throughout the year and to make Urea, Potash and DAP available in the State

Speaker: Ram Shiromani Verma — Shrawasti
Speech: Today, the farmers of the entire Prussia including my constituency Shrasti and Birampur Aspirational District are in distress and distress. Farmers are forced to sell their paddy crop to middlemen at ₹15-₹16 per kg. They are not getting the benefit of MSP. Paddy is grown in three seasons of the centre. Ignoring them permanently throughout the year is increasing the exploitation by middlemen. Even in the central cities, the purchase is very slow. In such a situation, farmers have to sell their hard-earned money at throwaway prices. Also, there is serious shortage of urea, DAP, potash, dye and fertilizer in many districts of North Prussia and Shei including Shrasti and Birampur. Farmers are wandering from door to door in search of food. Therefore, my request to the Government of India and the Government of Uttar Pradesh is that the grain centers should be declared permanent throughout the year, they should be expanded and centers should be established every 5-10 kilometers, so that the farmers can sell their produce whenever they want, they can get fair MSP, and they can be saved from the exploitation of middlemen. Also, adequate arrangements should be made for urea, potash, dye and DAP etc. (Inat) Re: Need for revival of proposed NTPC Thermal Power Plant at Katwa, West Bengal for productive utilization of acquired land

Speaker: Sharmila Sarkar — Bardhaman Purba(Sc)
Speech: The West Bengal Government handed over nearly 850 hectares of land in Katwa to NTPC for the setting up of two 660 MW super thermal power units and 100 acres of Government land was allotted free of cost. Land was acquired from nearly 4,000 farmers across Sreekhand, Debkundu, Koshigram and Churpuny mouzas of Katwa- I Block. There has never been any shortage of coal, water or transport facilities. The State Government even ensured coal supply through ECL and Panchami. Yet, for 14 years, the NTPC has left the entire site unused. The land is fenced with boundary walls and barbed wire, but lies abandoned, overgrown with weeds and turning into a wasteland. If the NTPC had no intention of setting up the project, it was not necessary to acquire and fence off such vast fertile land. Local farmers have been deprived for years, watching their livelihood turn into a wasteland. The people of Katwa urgently seek either the project’s revival or the return of their land. I request the Power Minister to intervene at the earliest. Re: Problems being faced by farmers due to shortage of Urea in the country

Speaker: Saugata Roy — Dum Dum
Speech: Indian farmers face severe hardships from urea shortages, leading to long queues, black marketing, overcharging, and distress during peak sowing seasons, threatening crop yields and food security, as demand outstrips subsidized supply, compounded by logistical issues, global price volatility, and dealer practices, forcing farmers to choose between crop failure and financial loss, despite Government efforts and promotion of alternatives like nano urea. Farmers, including women, queue from early morning or even overnight to get limited urea bags. There is no stringent efforts to check artificial scarcity & black marketing, dealers allegedly hoard stock, creating shortages, and sell above the subsidized MRP, sometimes forcing purchase of other products. Financial strain, farmers pay exorbitant rates in the black market (up to 10x MRP in some areas) or face crop loss, while dealers struggle with delayed subsidy reimbursements. Delayed fertilizer application stunts root growth and reduces yields, especially for Kharif crops, impacting food security and rural incomes. Global factors like dependence on imports exposes India to price shocks and geopolitical issues. In essence, the urea shortage creates a crisis where farmers are caught between agricultural failure and financial ruin, highlighting deep-seated issues in India's fertilizer sector. Government should intervene for immediate redressal farmers plights. Re: Need to increase the number of flight services between Salem and Chennai in Tamil Nadu

Speaker: T. M. Selvaganapathi — Salem
Speech: The airport at Salem is a tiny one, capable to operate only ATR-72 type aircraft. Flights from Salem are operated to only four cities viz. Chennai, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Kochi on limited basis. Since Salem airport is a shift airport, there are no flight operations during night. Chennai bound passengers from Salem and vice versa are increasing day by day, and therefore, there has been a demand to increase the flight services in between Salem-Chennai and Chennai-Salem. Similarly, flights to Bengaluru, Hyderabad and Kochi are operated only four days a week. Passengers demand that this should be made daily services to all these places. Flights to Kochi are being operated by Alliance Air. However, it has become a routine affair for the Alliance Air to cancel the flight to Kochi quite often, putting passengers in an awkward situation that they can neither go by road or by train. It should be stopped. Therefore, I urge upon the Government to increase the flight services to Chennai from Salem and also convert four days a week flight services to Bengaluru, Hyderabad and Kochi from Salem into daily services to all these places as well as enhance the basic facilities at the airport. (ends) Re: Need for execution of Dindigul-Lowercamp (Kumily) broad-gauge rail route project in Theni Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Thanga Tamil Selvan — Theni
Speech: I wish to raise a very important issue of 50 years long-pending dream scheme pertaining to my Theni Parliamentary constituency. The first one is Dindigul -Lowercamp (Kumily) broad-gauge route is only 134 Kms with the estimated budget of Rs. 2000/-crore which is easy to execute and profitable for both railways and public. By executing this route around 10 lakhs people from 03 Districts viz., Theni, Dindigiul and Iduki districts will be benefitted. Secondly, Lowercamp (Kumily)-Sabarimala Route is 70 kms only, but hard to execute because this route covers with wild life sanctuary, Mullaiperiyar Dam, hilly and forest area. If implemented this route, pilgrims from various parts of the country will be benefitted and their time will be saved to reach Sabarimala temple located in Kerala and the railway department will also earn lot of profit. This route is a shortest route to reach Sabarimala temple from Lowercamp (Kumily). In view of the above, I urge upon the Ministry of Railways to execute this Dindigul Lowercamp (Kumily) scheme first and then can be extended to Sabarimala from Lowercamp (Kurmily) which is good for public and easy for railway department to execute it. Re: Need for establishment of National Chilli Research Center in Palnadu district, Andhra Pradesh

Speaker: Lavu Sri Krishna Devarayalu — Narasaraopet
Speech: Urgent need to establish a National Chilli Research Center in Palnadu district, Andhra Pradesh. The Palnadu–Guntur region is India's largest and most globally recognized chilli-producing cluster, contributing significantly to national production and export supply chains. India’s chilli exports continue to rise sharply—in FY 2023-24, chilli exports reached US $1,508.94 million (approximately ₹12,600 crore), with export volumes crossing 6 lakh tonnes. This underscores India’s global dominance and the critical importance of strengthening scientific research to support this high-value crop. Yet, chilli farmers in Palnadu face multiple challenges: absence of structured research support, lack of high-yield and disease-resistant varieties, and reliance on unhygienic, labour-intensive post-harvest drying methods that reduce quality and export competitiveness. Earlier interventions in Parliament have highlighted these gaps, and the need for a specialized national center under ICAR has already been acknowledged. The Government of India has demonstrated strong commitment to spices research through initiatives like the Indo-Israel Center of Excellence for Vegetables & Spices, showing clear precedent for further central investment in this belt. I therefore urge the Hon’ble Union Agriculture Ministry to sanction a National Chilli Research Center in Palnadu, ensuring internationally benchmarked R&D support for lakhs of chilli farmers and strengthening India’s growing export footprint. Re: Need to provide stoppage of train numbers 15509/15510 and 13213/13214 at Tharbitia railway station in Supaul Parliamentary Constituency

Speaker: Dileshwar Kamait — Supaul
Speech: My constituency is located in Sapura district at Gayat Tharbantiya railway station in Samastipur Madhya Pradesh of East Central region between Sapura railway station and Nitagram. There is dense population around the station. The stoppage of Passenger Express train at the said station is extremely necessary in the public interest which will benefit thousands of people and will increase the revenue of the train and will facilitate the local people in commuting to Patna, the capital of Bihar and the district main thoroughfare. Therefore, Honorable Railway Minister is requested to stop 2 minutes of train number 15509/15510 Nitigram to Patna, Rajyarani Express and train number 13213/13214 Jogbani-Saharsa Express at the station of Tharbinatiya of Samastipur Madhya Pradesh via East Central Railway in public interest. Go. (Inat) Re: Need to create a separate category for DNT community in Caste Census

Speaker: Devusinh Chauhan — Kheda
Speech: Under the "Degraded Tribes Act" enacted by the British government in 1871, many nomadic tribal communities - such as Dhangar, Gajuyar, Dom, Bajara etc. - were collectively declared as 'criminal tribes'. This inhuman law has pushed these communities towards social exclusion, oppression and deep marginalization for generations. In 1952, on the recommendation of the Aygar Committee, this law was repealed and 'denotified' was made mandatory for these communities, but provisions like Habitual Offenders Act and Wildlife Protection Act of 1972 and Animal Protection Act of 2006 still have an adverse effect on their traditional means of livelihood. Even today, deontide, Ghumtu and non-DNT communities continue to struggle with severe social discrimination, lack of permanent residence, limited access to education and health services, and extreme poverty. It is clear from the study that the school dropout rate among the girls of these communities is very high, more than half of the families are forced to live on less than ₹ 50,000 per year, most of the families do not even have caste certificate, identity card or ration card, due to which they remain deprived of the benefits of government schemes. Their economic activities are informal, micro-economic and generate very low income, which hinders their social progress. In the caste census, the DNT community should be counted in a separate and clear category, a sub-category based reservation system should be made for these communities, and a dedicated central ministry should be established for their overall exit, promotion and rehabilitation. (Inat) Re: Reported fall in rural employment generation in Andhra Pradesh under MGNREGS

Speaker: P. V. Midhun Reddy — Rajampet
Speech: MGNREGA MIS data recently compiled covering April to September 2025 shows a significant and worrying fall in rural employment generation in Andhra Pradesh during the first half of the 2025–26 financial year. Person days dropped by 13.6 per cent, which is notably steeper than the national average decline of 10.4 per cent. Household participation also fell from 42.79 lakh to 40.74 lakh. The data further highlights disproportionate impacts on vulnerable social groups. Person days for Scheduled Caste workers declined by 18.7 per cent and for Scheduled Tribe workers by 17 per cent, compared with an 11.3 per cent fall among other categories. The decline in work availability has translated into reduced household earnings. Average income generated per MGNREGA household fell by 4.8 per cent, from ₹10,695 in 2024–25 to ₹10,178 in 2025–26. I urge the Ministry of Rural Development to urgently intervene by expanding labor budgets, addressing Aadhaar-related access barriers and ensuring timely wage payments to stabilize rural livelihoods and prevent further distress. (ends) Re:NeedtoreleasethependingcentralfundsunderJalJeevanMissiontoexpedite completionofdrinkingwaterprojectsinSanglidistrict,Maharashtra.

Speaker: Vishal Patil — Sangli
Speech: I wish to draw the attention of the Government to the severe disruption in rural drinking-water projects in Sangli district due to the delayed and stalled release of Central funds under the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM). Several States, including Maharashtra, have reported that allocations due for the current financial year have not been released on time, resulting in contractors stopping work, pipelines lying incomplete, and villages that were close to receiving Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTCs) now left without assured access to potable water. In the Sangli District, financial disruption has also created mistrust at the panchayat level, with many vendors and contractors refusing to take up further work without clarity on payments. This undermines the credibility of a flagship national programme. Schools, anganwadis and health centers in affected areas are also facing irregular supply, compromising hygiene and public health. Such issues are undermining the Mission’s core objective of providing safe, reliable, household-level tap water. I therefore urge the Hon'ble Minister of Jal Shakti to immediately release the pending Central share of JJM funds, clear accumulated arrears, and enable the concerned States,includingMaharashtra,toresumehaltedworkswithoutdelay. Re:NeedtoreconstitutetheFareFixationCommittee(FFC)forfarerevisionofBangalore MetroRailCorporationLtd(BMRCL)

Speaker: Tejasvi Surya — Bangalore South
Speech: I highlight serious concerns regarding the recent fare revision implemented by Bangalore Metro Rail Corporation Limited (BMRCL) based on the recommendations of the Fare Fixation Committee (FFC) constituted under Section 34 of the Metro Railway(O&M)Act,2002.Therevision,whichhasraisedthemaximumfarefrom₹60to₹90,appears to be the result of significant errors in the application of the prescribed fare-fixation formula. Preliminary analysis of the FFC report indicates that BMRCL has used an incorrect base year, applied inconsistent maintenance-cost calculations, and adopted inaccurate route-kilometre figures. These deviations have substantially inflated the percentage increase in maintenance and administration costs, leading to an exaggerated projection of fare escalation. As a result, Bengaluru's metro fares are the highest in the country even as Delhi Metro, which operates a larger network, maintains a maximum fare of ₹64. Such steep fares, when combined with first- and last-mile costs, make metro travel more expensive than private transport for many commuters, defeating the goal of shifting people toward public transport. Given these anomalies and the significant burden on Bengaluru’s citizens, I urge the Government to reconstitute the FFC to reassess the revision and ensureatransparent,accurateandcommuter-friendlyfarestructure. While the Bill talks about the safety and waste management, it does not clearly mention how radioactive waste will be safely stored and monitored over the long term. Nuclear waste remains dangerous for decades, even centuries, but the law does not clearly fix responsibility for environmental clean up once the plant stops functioning. There are no clear legal duties on who will pay for and manage long-term waste storage and land restoration. On account of this, there is a real concern that the cost and risk of nuclear waste management may be pushed on to future governments and future generations. Such long-lasting environmental risks require clearer legal safeguards and stronger accountability. In this Bill, there is a gap in emergency preparedness at the local level. The Bill gives strong emergency powers to the Central Government, but it says very little about preparing people on the ground to handle a nuclear emergency. District administrations, local hospitals, disaster response teams, and nearby communities are the first to face the impact of any accident. However, the Bill does not clearly talk about training, medical preparedness, emergency drills, or public awareness programs in areas around nuclear plants. Without strong local capacity and planning, central control alone may not be enough during a crisis. This gap raises concern about whether our emergency response system is truly ready to protect the lives of citizens. With these comments and suggestions, I support this, Bill. Thank you. at 1517

Speaker: Anup Dhotre — Akola
Speech: Thank you, hon. Chairperson Sir, for allowing me to speak on this important Bill, that is, the Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Bill, 2025, also known as the SHANTI Bill. Hon. Chairperson Sir, it is our hon. Prime Minister's target to achieve 100 Gigawatts of nuclear power capacity by the year 2047. It has been observed that cities like Delhi located near thermal power plants experience nearly 30 per cent to 40 per cent of the total pollution due to emissions from coal-based thermal power plants. Additionally, coal transportation to thermal power stations is predominantly carried out through Indian Railways, accounting for nearly 49 per cent of total freight movement. This heavy reliance on coal freight occupies a substantial portion of Indian Railways capacity, leading to constraints on passenger train services and the movement of other essential goods. Sir, nuclear power today is no longer merely a source of electricity; it is a strategic national asset. During 2024-25, India generated 56.68 billion units of nuclear electricity, thereby preventing the emission of about 56.68 million tonnes of carbon dioxide in a single year. With one of the lowest life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions, nuclear energy strongly supports India's commitment to achieving net-zero emissions by 2070. Nuclear reactors produce hydrogen by using methods such as electrolysis and advanced thermochemical cycles or hybrid methods. Hydrogen is a fuel for automobile industry with zero emissions. Sir, nuclear power is also cost-effective and commercially viable. Some of the cheapest electricity in India today is generated by nuclear plants. The Tarapur Atomic Power Station Units 1 and 2 generate electricity at less than one rupee per unit which is highly competitive. Moreover, our nuclear power operator, NPCIL, is a AAA-rated company that consistently earns commercial profits, clearly dispelling the notion that nuclear power in India is not an economically viable option. However, the cost of establishing a nuclear power plant in India is approximately Rs. 20 crore per MW. Therefore, private sector participation and investment are essential. Sir, this Bill replaces outdated legislation with a single, comprehensive, and future-ready framework. It significantly strengthens our regulatory oversight, rationalizes civil liability provisions, and enables responsible participation while fully safeguarding national interests. It establishes a strengthened Atomic Energy Regulatory Board with clearly defined powers for licensing, safety authorization, inspection, enforcement, emergency response, and public outreach, thereby ensuring transparency and enhancing public confidence. Sir, the BARC has developed TAG-24, which stands for Trombay-Akola Groundnut-24, a popular and high-yielding groundnut variety, through mutation and recombination breeding at its Trombay facilities. This variety was developed by crossing mutant derivatives of the Spanish-improved groundnut variety and was achieved in collaboration with Dr. Panjabrao Deshmukh Krishi Vidyapeeth (PDKV), the agricultural university, serving my constituency, Akola. This was a successful collaboration, which stands as a fine example of how nuclear science and agriculture research can directly benefit farmers at grassroot level. I would also like to draw your attention to the role of Baba Atomic Research Center (BARC) in the field of agriculture applications. BARC has developed a radiation technology that significantly enhances the shelf life of agronomical as well as horticultural crops through controlled use of radioactive sources such as Cobalt- 60. This technology helps reduce post-harvest losses, improves food security and increases farmers' income. I, therefore, request necessary enabling provisions and supportive amendments to be incorporated in the Bill to promote wider application of such nuclear and radiation-based technologies in agriculture, which would be of immense benefits to the farmers. In conclusion, I support this Bill. This landmark legislation centers India's energy security, accelerates climate action, promotes technological leadership and ensures public safety. I strongly support this Bill, and urge the August House to do the same in the larger national interest. Thank you, Sir. Honorable Chairman (Shri Silip Shaikia): Shri Hanuman Beniwal. Speaker: Gowaal Kagada Padavi — Nandurbar(St)
Speech: Thank you, Chairperson, Sir. I directly want to speak about the Bill that is the VB–G RAM G Bill. This Bill may look constitutionally tenable, may be constitutionally tenable, but it is vulnerable. It is not per se unconstitutional or lacks legislative competence, but multiple provisions are likely to be struck down or read down by courts because of the dilution of the fundamental rights, violating established constitutional principles, and contradicting binding Supreme Court judgments. Employment and rural development fall under Entry 23 of List 3, that is the Concurrent List as well as the Entry 20 of List 3. Parliament does not have the power to enact this law. So the challenge is not about lack of power, but about how the power is exercised. MNREGA is not just a scheme, it is a rights-based welfare legislation. The VB–G RAM G Bill replaces a demand-driven legal entitlement. With a budget-capped executive control program, this directly triggers constitutional scrutiny. As the Supreme Court judgment in Olga Tellis states, the right to livelihood is an integral part of Article 21 of the Constitution. In Swaraj Abhiyan vs. Union of India, delayed or denied MNREGA wages violates Article 21. This VB–G RAM G Bill is vulnerable because it caps unemployment irrespective of distress, allows suspension of work for up to 60 days, shifts unemployment allowance burdens to the States at the 60-40 ratio, and makes employment contingent on budget ceilings, not needed. This is in violation of the doctrine of non-retrogression. Once the State creates a welfare right, it cannot regress arbitrarily. In the State of Punjab vs. Ram Lubhaya Bagga case, the hon. Supreme Court says that welfare measures, once institutionalized, cannot be withdrawn arbitrarily. Similarly, in Navtej Singh Johar's case, fundamental rights jurisprudence prohibits backsliding. I want to say that this is violative of Article 14 as well. I will quickly finish it up. It is because there is an assault on federalism as well as the basic structure of the Constitution because it shifts financial burden on the States, retains rulemaking ceilings, audits with the Center and leaves States accountable without authority. I just want to finish by stating that there is weak accountability and social audits. This is against Article 21, the rule of law. As well as, there is likely to have a judicial outcome that could be striking down of suspension of work clauses, arbitrary budget caps, and unilateral cost shifting. In the end, I just want to say one line. The VB–G RAM G Bill may have legislative competence, but it fails the constitutional test of dignity, equality, non-regression, federalism, and right to livelihood under Article 21. Thank you. (ends) at 0043

(Iqtat) Re: Reported recent scientific study finding about the country losing its sunshine due to aerosol and particulate emissions leading to polluted air

Speaker: Naveen Jindal — Kurukshetra
Speech: I rise to draw the attention of this august House and the Hon'ble Minister of Environment, Forest and Climate Change to a recent scientific study published in Scientific Reports (Nature Portfolio) titled "India losing its sunshine to cloud cover, bad air." The study, conducted by scientists from Banaras Hindu University, the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology, and the India Meteorological Department, finds that India's sunshine hours have declined consistently over the past three decades. Based on data from 20 weather stations (1988–2018), the steepest annual drop around 13.1 hours per year has been recorded across the northern inland region, the Himalayan belt, and the west coast. Researchers attribute this decline to persistent cloud cover and increasing air pollution, particularly aerosols from industrial emissions, vehicular exhaust, and biomass burning. These aerosols and clouds together have reduced solar radiation reaching the surface by nearly 40%, threatening India's agricultural productivity and renewable energy ambitions under the National Solar Mission. I propose that the Government should conduct a detailed assessment of regional sunlight trends, integrate solar irradiance monitoring into environmental data systems, and strengthen measures to curb aerosol and particulate emissions to ensure clean skies and energy security. (Iqtat) Re: Need to implement measures to combat air pollution in Mumbai

Speaker: Varsha Gaikwad — Mumbai North Central
Speech: The increasing air pollution in Mumbai has now become a serious health problem. In many parts of the city, AQI continues to remain between 150-200, while in areas like Vennaar and Wadala it has reached dangerous levels above 300. According to IQAir and media reports, Mabuei has become the most polluted city in the world. The Lancet and the Air Quality Life Index show that due to pollution, the average life expectancy of a person is decreasing by about 3.7 years, and about 1.7 million people die every year across the region due to air pollution. Violations of rules in the Dharavi Redevelopment Project, felling of more than 1,700 trees for Sadhu Gram Colony in Nakshak and felling of more than 2,000 trees for Metro car shed in Aarey Colony have caused huge damage to the green cover, further deteriorating the air quality. The Government and the Ministry of Urban Development should enforce strict control on construction site dust. At hazardous AQI levels, it is necessary to immediately stop construction, make regular water sprinkling, air-dust guns, and wheel-wash systems mandatory. If decisive steps are not taken now, there is a serious risk of a Delhi-like situation in Mumbai too. (Iqtat) Re: Need for a waste to energy plant and clearance of garbage dump mound at Dadumajra in Chandigarh

Speaker: Manish Tewari — Chandigarh
Speech: I wish to raise the matter of repeated delays in clearing the Dadumajra garbage dump in Chandigarh, despite commitments made before the National Green Tribunal and in earlier Parliamentary replies. The Government initially assured completion of bio-remediation by December 2024, later revising it to July 2025. However, the Chandigarh Municipal Corporation has again extended the deadline to 30 November, contradicting earlier assurances and causing concern among affected residents. Despite substantial financial allocations and claims that two waste heaps have already been cleared, progress remains inadequate. A large quantum of legacy waste is still untreated, and fresh municipal waste continues to accumulate daily, compounding environmental hazards. Additionally, between 2004 and 2025, no fully functional waste-to-energy plant has been commissioned in Chandigarh—particularly at Dadumajra—despite repeated proposals and chronic waste buildup. Repeated delays have raised serious public health, environmental, and governance concerns for residents living near the site. I urge the Government to ensure immediate and time-bound clearance of the garbage mountain through transparent monitoring, accountability mechanisms, and establishment of a functional waste-to-energy plant. Steps must also be taken to prevent recurrence of such landfill crises, including strict enforcement of municipal waste segregation and regular review of landfill management protocols. Re: Request for intervention regarding policy gaps and improper implementation of the Revamped Distribution Center Scheme (RDSS) Ms. Iqra Choudhary (Kairana): I would like to draw the attention of the Honorable Electricity Minister to the problem of poor implementation of the Revamped Distribution Center Scheme (RDSS) due to policy gaps and improper implementation across the sector, especially in my subsidy area. I want to draw attention towards the serious difficulties being faced by the people of Rana. In many villages and colonies, LT lines are not being extended and consumers are being told that such work is not allowed under the scheme. As a result, households wishing to have legal electricity connections do not get supply as the line does not reach their homes. In many unauthorized and low-income colonies, residents are being asked to pay for poles, wires and electric line extensions at the rate of around Rs 20 per meter, which is far more than a poor family can afford. The situation is that the responsibility is put on the original developers of these colonies but today these developers cannot be identified because most of the colonies are illegal due to which the common residents are not entitled to a valid connection at the government expense due to this rule of the government and they find it very expensive to get the connection at their own expense. This has become a big problem, for this the government needs to change its policy so that the residents of illegal colonies can get connections at low cost. Without this, people seeking a legitimate electricity connection are being pushed towards electricity theft. Additionally, the labor and material rates released by the government under RDSS are much higher than market prices, making the process even more ineffective. I urge the Government to make these rates ticket compatible, exempt LT line work under RCSS fund and bring a special relief policy for unauthorized colonies so that genuine consumers can get legitimate electricity connections without any unnecessary burden. (Iqtat) Re: Need to reduce the number of liquor shops in the country and implement strict liquor policy

Speaker: Bijendra Singh — Chandauli
Speech: I would like to draw your attention to the opening of a large number of liquor shops in the name of increasing the liquor revenue. Especially in North Prussia, the liquor vends are increasing at the rate of about 20-30% every year, which is extremely alarming. Due to government pressure, many liquor vends, disregarding the prescribed standards, are opening liquor vends among the poor and vulnerable communities – the poor, the poor, the Scheduled Tribes, the outcastes, the extremely backward classes, the tribal and squatter communities, who are in violation of the current liquor policy. It is against the law. Due to this, incidents like violence, atrocities on women, domestic violence, rape and murder have increased in the villages. Additionally, there has been an adverse impact on children's education, health and overall development. Therefore, I request the government to reduce the number of liquor vends in Prussia/Shame. This strict standard should be implemented that any liquor vend should be established at a distance of at least 1 kilometer from the village or residential colony, and full restrictions should be imposed on such vends in community settlements. (Iqtat) Re: Need to conduct a safety audit of the Tejas fighter aircraft SHRIMATI RACHNA BANERJEE (HOOGHLY): Firstly, I want to convey my deepest condolences to the family of Wing Commander Namansh Syal, who lost his life in the recent Tejas Aircraft crash. This devastating incident has claimed the life of a young 34 year old. Further, it has raised a realm of questions as this is the second crash in less than two years. What is needed is an immediate and thorough investigation. We must urgently determine the cause of these repeated crashes. While a Court of Inquiry has been constituted, we seek assurance on the floor of the house regarding a full transparent investigation. Serious concerns arise regarding pre-flight preparedness and maintenance protocols. The government must clarify the aircraft's maintenance record, compliance with safety checks, and any negligence involved. It is extremely important to know whether the ejection system was functioning or not. Further, we request the Government to conduct a safety audit of the entire Tejas fleet so that we would not lose any young pilots in such incidents. To maintain public trust and protect the future of our pilots we demand a clear, transparent investigation and request the Government to make the findings of the Court of Inquiry public as soon as possible. (ends) Re: Need to release the disaster relief funds due to West Bengal at the earliest SUSHRI MAHUA MOITRA (KRISHNANAGAR): This noticed that while West Bengal reels under multiple disasters no comprehensive measure has been extended to address the grave situation in the State. While the Center released ₹13,603.20 crore from the SDRF and ₹2,189.28 crore from the NDRF to other states this year, Bengal has been left out. During the river erosion, flood and landslide in September 2024, financial damage to the extent of Rs. 4233.43 crores were assessed, yet no assistance was received from the Union Government. Similarly, no financial assistance was received from the Union during the cyclone Remal and severe cyclonic storm Dana in 2024. Around Rs. 44,000 Cr. is owed by the Union Government on account of the damages suffered from Bulbul, Amphan and Yass cyclones. Overall, a total of Rs. 53,696 crores are pending as disaster management funds from the Union Government and ultimately it is the ordinary citizen who pays the price. Hence, I urge the Government to stand by the people of West Bengal and release the pending disaster relief funds immediately. Re: Need to review the decision to grant approval to Madurai and Coimbatore metro rail projects in Tamil Nadu

Speaker: Ganapathi P. Raj Kumar — Coimbatore
Speech: The Tamil Nadu Government has submitted a detailed project report through Chennai Metro Rail Corporation in February, 2014 to the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development for the implementation of metro rail projects 'Temple City' Madurai and 'Manchester of South India Coimbatore. In Madurai, it has been decided to implement a distance of 31.93 km between Thirumangalam Othakadai at a cost of Rs 11,360 crore and in Coimbatore, from Avinashi Road to Karumathampatti, and from Ukkadam to Valiyampalayam section on Sathyamangalam Road, for a distance of 39 km, at a cost of Rs 10,740 crore. Hoping to receive the Central Government's approval soon, Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank officials have completed the survey in Madurai and Coimbatore for speedy implementation of metro rail projects in these cities Later, as requested by the Union Government a revised report with additional documents was also submitted by the Tamil Nadu Government 10 months ago. In this situation, the Union Ministry has returned the project reports of the above two metro rail projects to the Tamil Nadu government citing not enough population as per the 2011 census, and according to the 2017 rules, the metro rail project will be implemented only in cities with a population of more than 20 lakhs. It is pertinent to mention here that the Central Government has given approval for metro rail projects in cities including Kanpur and Agra in Uttar Pradesh and Nagpur in Maharashtra, which have a population of less than 2 million, but has denied it to Tamil Nadu, Hence, I urge upon the Union Government to reconsider the decision and grant approval at the earliest as approved for Kanpur, Agra and Nagpur to implement the metro rail projects in Temple City Madurai and 'Manchester of South India' Coimbatore. which is essential for future development to Madurai and Coimbatore. Re: Need to maintain balance between compulsory licensing and voluntarily licensing in the field of Medicines under WTO/TRIPS agreement

Speaker: Mathukumilli Bharat — Visakhapatnam
Speech: I rise to draw attention to a pressing challenge at the intersection of public health and intellectual property in our country — the delicate balance between compulsory licensing and voluntary licensing under the TRIPS framework. Since adopting TRIPS-compliant patent laws in 2005, India has relied predominantly on voluntary licensing to expand access to medicines, as seen in HIV/AIDS therapies, Hepatitis-C treatment, and even the rapid scale-up of Covishield. These agreements have brought prices down dramatically when originator companies were willing to cooperate. Yet India’s experience also shows the limits of a voluntary-only approach. Delays or restrictive terms, as witnessed with remdesivir during COVID-19, leave patients vulnerable. Compulsory licensing, though used only once in the 2012 Nexavar case, delivered a 97% price reduction and remains a vital public health safeguard. Recent developments — including the Natco–Roche Evrysdi decision and debates around the India–U.K. The trade agreement affirms that our TRIPS flexibilities remain intact. The path forward is not choosing VL or CL, but strengthening both. India must preserve a credible compulsory licensing option while using voluntary licensing as a first resort, supported by transparency and multilateral mechanisms. For millions of patients, this balance is essential to ensure affordability without undermining innovation. Re: Need to declare Emperor Ashoka's birthday as national holiday

Speaker: Sunil Kushwaha — Valmiki Nagar
Speech: I have drawn the attention of the Honorable Minister that every day of the birthday of Emperor Ashoka, the day of Hindi month Chaitra Shukla Paksha has been celebrated as Ashokami. Work has been done to convey the public welfare impact of Emperor Ashoka's rule, policy scriptures and architectural scriptures to the lower reaches. Should be known. The “Ashoka Chakra” in the national flag is the national symbol of India. The Honorable Chief Minister of Bihar has declared the birth day of Emperor Ashoka as a national holiday, which is highly commendable. Therefore, I request the Honorable Minister to honor the glorious reign of Emperor Ashoka. Looking at the history, please recognize the national mountain by declaring his birth day (Ashokami) in Hindi month Chaitra Shukla Paksha Ammi as a national holiday every year and give momentum to this great mountain. (Iqtat) Re: Need to sanction additional modem rakes for Mumbai Suburban railway in Maharashtra SHRI SANJAY DINA PATIL (MUMBAI NORTH-EAST): The Mumbai Suburban Railway is the lifeline of our financial capital, carrying between 75 lakh and 1 crore passengers every single day. Yet this lifeline is stretched to a breaking point. Chronic overcrowding, driven by an acute shortage of train rakes, has turned daily travel into a serious safety hazard for millions of citizens. The tragic Mumbra incident of June 2025 is a painful reminder. Two local trains crossed on a sharp curve, and due to extreme overcrowding, commuters standing on the footboards were thrown onto the tracks. Five innocent lives were lost, and more than fifteen were grievously injured. These were preventable deaths. It is our collective responsibility to ensure that such tragedies never recur. I urge the Ministry of Railways to immediately sanction additional rakes for the Mumbai suburban network, with priority given to modern rakes equipped with automatic closing doors. This single intervention will significantly reduce footboard travel and save countless lives. Mumbai's commuters deserve safety, dignity, and reliable transport. I request the Government to act with urgency. Re: Widening of Sahebganj - Dumka National Highway

Speaker: Vishnu Dayal Ram — Palamau(Sc)
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the Honorable Road Transport and Highways Minister towards Sahebgi-Damu National Highway. In this National Highway, the main road from Kathikud to Damu has become very dirty due to the continuous movement of coal and heavy vehicles. Due to abundance of heavy coal loaded trucks on this route, the condition of the road is getting worse day by day, due to which it is becoming shorter day by day. Many times trucks enter the house with excessive speed, causing huge damage to life and property. A permanent solution to this problem is necessary. First of all, the width of this road should be increased so that there is no hindrance in the movement of heavy vehicles as well as common people. Apart from this, a separate alternate route (bypass road) should be constructed for coal transportation, so that the safety of local citizens can be minimized and the pressure on the main route can be reduced. (Iqtat) Re: Need for immediate repatriation and assistance to Indian crew stranded in UAE

Speaker: Sudhakar Singh — Buxar
Speech: The captain of the ship MT SUDARSHAN, an Indian citizen, has been stranded at Hamaria Port in UAE for the last 18 months without pay, without passport and no legal aid. He was kept in inhumane conditions at the Abu Dhabi CID office for 8 days, with no proper food, no medical attention and no help from the Indian Embassy. Meanwhile here his wife is seriously ill and the family is suffering from financial and mental distress. Under the Maritime Labor Convention, 2006 and the Maritime Workers Safety Rules of the Special Ministry, it is the responsibility of the Government of India to ensure the safety of Indian maritime workers trapped in seas. I urge the Government to immediately intervene and ensure the early return of all the Indian crew, including the Captain, to the UAE Government. Get his passport recovered, meet him directly, provide him full legal and humanitarian support and file a case of “Seafarer Abandonment” against the owner/owner and suspend his license and provide immediate financial assistance to his family and medical assistance to his ailing wife. Be pure and pure. (Iktat) Re: Need for immediate release of Central share of pending post-matric and other scholarships to SCs/STs in Jharkhand

Speaker: Pradeep Kumar Singh — Araria
Speech: The post-matric scholarship delivery process in Jharkhand has been seriously disrupted in the last three years (2022-23, 2023-24 and 2024-25). This has had an adverse impact especially on the economically weaker students belonging to Scheduled Tribes, Scheduled Castes, Backward Classes and Minorities – who have to cope with fees, examinations and academic rigors and many students are dropping out. Have reached the stage of. This is contrary to the universal values ​​of right to education and social justice. Therefore, the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment and the Ministry of Rural Affairs are requested to immediately release the Central Share earmarked for Post-Matric Scholarship and other Scholarship Schemes and make public the year-wise and category-wise beneficiaries and payment status. A time bound permanent mechanism should be developed to ensure that such a situation does not happen in future. I once again urge the Government to treat this issue as a matter of utmost priority and take immediate intervention and streamline the payment of scholarships without any compromise. Speaker: A. Raja — Nilgiris(Sc)
Speech: Mr. Chairman, Sir, the House is witnessing a strange and distinct occasion, which has never occurred in the world. No doubt, irrespective of political colour, when the song Vande Mataram is played, everybody stands and gives his or her respect to show that we are in oneness. Why did I say it is very strange. I have come across a few countries, probably Denmark and New Zealand, that have one more national anthem. But I have not come across any country where a country has two songs under two different nomenclatures with new definitions. One is the national anthem and another is the national song. It is very strange and peculiar to the rest of the world. Then I wanted to search on Google, including the Oxford dictionary, to see what is meant by an anthem and what is meant by a song. An anthem is a song that has special importance for a country. What is meant by a song? A short musical piece with words meant to be sung. Then another explanation that has been offered in the Oxford dictionary itself is that a song could become an anthem meant for a classless society. So, an anthem has a larger motto and a larger ambition to have inclusive nationalism, so that our forefathers distinguished both poems – one for the national anthem and another for the national song. In a country like ours, India, when we are divided by caste, religion, linguistic differences, and territorial differences, yes, we need a national anthem to unite us, to have a unified force in our mind that this is our country. In order to cultivate such patriotism and national conscience, one can understand that there should be a national anthem. But what is the need for a national song? So many correspondences were held to arrive at these two songs with equal status. I am aware that the song Vande Mataram is deeply attached to the freedom struggle. As put by the Prime Minister, and friends such as Shri Gogoi, Shrimati Ghosh from West Bengal, and Shri Akhilesh, all this shows that we have a unanimous voice to celebrate this present form of Vande Mataram, but that does not prohibit us from questioning the genesis of this song, which has been tabled and named as the national song. I have seen the Prime Minister’s speeches many times over the past 11 years. Of course, the hon. Prime Minister is fond of a few words. One of his best inclinations is towards the word ‘appeasement’. Without ‘appeasement’, the Prime Minister’s speech will not end. Whether it is the Red Fort or a BJP conference, the tone, voice, and vocabulary are one and the same – appeasement, appeasement, appeasement! And one more is nepotism, nepotism, nepotism! He is entitled to that. But what kind of appeasement was done as far as Vande Mataram is concerned? I too am fond of the word ‘appeasement’. But we are travelling in opposite direction as far as the Vande Mataram song is concerned. Vande Mataram was popularly acclaimed as the national song, written by the respected Bankim Chandra Chatterjee way back in 1870. But it has given rise to intense contestation in terms of explicit idolatry and religious antagonism. We have attachment to our own language, culture, and regionalism. That does not mean everything should be glorified without seeing the documents and the credentials of the history. Sir, fortunately, Shri Bankim Chandra Chatterjee did not know what type of contestation took place over the national song Vande Mataram since he died before the political struggle started over the song. Sir, as such, explanation is not possible to give in this forum about what he did during the composition of Vande Mataram. Sir, let me quote a few sentences from the Prime Minister’s speech when he spoke on the 150th celebration of the song. It was held when the storm relief release was done by the Prime Minister. I quote, “In Anandamath, when Santan Bhavannath sings Vande Mataram, another character argues, “we get inspiration from Vande Mataram”. But today, Mother India has 140 crore children. He has got 280 crore arms. More than 60 per cent of these are young. This power belongs to Ma Bharti. What is impossible for us today? What can stop us from fulfilling the original dream of Vande Mataram? He further states that this song was subjected to division by cutting off two stanzas which sowed the seed for the partition. Sir, I want to ask four questions upon the speech that has been delivered by the Prime Minister. One is, who did the division in the Vande Mataram? I would also like to know whether the division of the Vande Mataram led to the division of the country. Then he said, this crucial aspect has to be dealt with now. I quote, the crucial side of the division. What is the crucial division to know now? This is my second question. In his speech, he stated that a challenging thinking of such a division exists even today. I want to ask this House. The Prime Minister is saying that a thinking of division still exists in the country today. Where is it? With whom is it? The last question, what is the original dream of Vande Mataram? All these four questions have arisen to me in my mind out of the speech that has been delivered by the Prime Minister. Sir, let me start with Gandhiji. The Government still pretends that they are respecting Gandhiji as the Father of the Nation. Of course, sometimes his name is anathema to the Treasury Benches. Sir, Gandhiji not only praised the Vande Mataram, but also he did not hesitate to put encomium to the author too. Sir, Gandhiji said, “In 1915, we have sung a beautiful national song on hearing which all of us sprang to our feet”. “… The poet has lavished all the adjectives we possibly could to describe Mother India. It is for you and me to make good the claim that the poet has advanced on behalf of his Motherland”. So, this is the encomium which was given by Gandhi ji to the author of the poem in the year 1915. Now, please come into 1940. After 25 years, Mahatma Gandhi was distinctly defensive about Vande Mataram when he said: “That was not a religious cry, but a purely political cry. It should never be chanted to insult or offend the Muslims”. What happened to Gandhi ji between 1915 and 1940? What happened to this National Song between 1915 and 1940? What are all the elements that pushed Gandhi ji to praise the song in 1915 and what are all the events held between 1915 and 1940 for him to say that the song should not be permitted to be sung? If the elements and events between 1915 and 1940s are correlated or synchronized, then the riddles wrapped upon this National Song ‘Vande Mataram’ can be unfolded. Very few points, I want to say in another angle. Anushilan Samiti, a secret revolutionary organization was founded in Bengal in the year 1902 aiming for independence through armed struggle. They were being called as ‘Viplabis’. The Professor may be aware about it. ‘Viplabis’, the militant group, formed themselves to achieve Independence of India in 1902. The greatest proponents of elevating the Vande Mataram to the status of National Song was done by these ‘Viplabis’. The secret documents were seized by the British Government to know about the activities of this Association where it has been disclosed that in the ‘Viplabis’ Muslims were not permitted to do struggle activities. When a question was asked, the reply was sent by the Samiti, and I quote: “Vows taken at the time of admission as members of the organization were sacred to the Hindus, but not the Muslims”. The poem is Vande Mataram; struggle is national struggle for the country; a group was formed; but Muslims were prohibited from chanting Vande Mataram. So, the inference can be that Vande Mataram is a religious thing. Right or wrong, such a space was given to the people and to a community. Its aim was independence, and its utterance prejudiced the Muslims. Accordingly, between 1905 and 1908, the Home Department revealed that when prayer was going on in the mosque in the State of Bengal, Hindu processionists -- while crossing the mosque, when the prayer was being done by the Muslims -- shouted Vande Mataram, which created enmity. It is documented by the British Government. Still, it is being saved and it is available for perusal. 1424 hours (Shri Dilip Saikia in the Chair) In 1907, again anonymous pamphlets were printed on lurid red paper and distributed, which claims that Muslims should not sing Vande Mataram and no Muslim should join the Swadeshi Movement. Sir, such events increased between 1902 and 1915. What prompted it and how it went? The unrest and disturbances occurred in various places including Bengal, when the matter was referred to the House of Commons. The House of Commons discussed these issues in detail as to why the song Vande Mataram was creating a communal conflict and who was responsible for that. The discussion took place on 16th July, 1907. An appeal was made by Aurobindo Ghosh with a broader sense which I want to re-quote again – “What we want is that Hindus and Muslims both should bring the Swaraj in unison and concert”. This appeal was done by Aurobindo Ghosh, having come across that there were disturbances communally between Hindus and Muslims because of the Vande Mataram chanting. The fault is not with the song, I will come to that later. According to them, it is meant for Hindus only, prohibiting Muslims in the struggle. So, divide starts there. Prime Minister asks, “Who did the division?” Prime Minister asks, “Who did the division of Vande Mataram song?” the division was done by your forefathers, not by Muslims. Sir, one more aspect is there. It may be a little bit painful for the West Bengal people, please bear with me, but naked history must be placed before this House. HON. CHAIRPERSON (SHRI DILIP SAIKIA): Raja Ji, what do you mean by “your forefathers” What do you mean by that? … HON. CHAIRPERSON: You can ask questions. It should be, “our forefathers”. You can ask questions. … HON. CHAIRPERSON: It should be mentioned like, “our forefathers”. … HON. CHAIRPERSON: Raja Ji, please, please be seated. …

Speaker: A. Raja — Nilgiris(Sc)
Speech: Sir, this type of conflicts which happened in Bengal and other areas including UP went on to the extent to investigate the character of the author. What happened? The collection of Shri Bankim Chandra Chatterjee were put into test, including the novel, Anandamath, from where the song, Vande Mataram, was inserted later. Tanika Sarkar, the famous historical researcher has said about the collection of the Bankim Chandra Chatterjee including Anandamath. He wrote many books. These were collected in the name of Bankim Rajbali. About the collection including Anandamath, Tanika Sarkar observed what recitals are available in this collection, including Anandamath. The conversation was held. It is very sensitive. I quote: “Unless we drive out these dirty bastards, Hindus will be ruined. When shall we level the mosques to the ground and raise the Ram Madhav temple in their place?” Who is the “bastard”? Silence. These are the words from which one may clearly infer their intended meaning. Sir, not only this. Again, in the same novel, describing how our Muslim ruler supposedly protects us, I quote: “We lost our religion; we lost our caste; we lost our honour and family name; and now we are on the verge of losing even our lives. How can Hinduism endure unless we drive out this dissolute swine?” Such observations, contemplated within the literature of Bankim Chandra, gave rise to animosity. Sir, I want to bring one more incident to the attention of this House. I make no assertion on the author. In Bengal, Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar raised his voice for women’s education. I believe the Chair is familiar with his name. He advocated women’s education, the abolition of polygamy, and widow remarriage. He submitted a memorandum with 2,000 signatures to the then Governor General of Bengal. But it was countered by the author of this song, who collected 28,000 signatures asserting that these reforms were against Hindu customs. Therefore, there is reason to believe indeed, reason to conclude that Vande Mataram is not merely a set of stanzas, nor is it directed solely against the British, but also against Muslims. These are the reasons I am placing before you. Sir, Shri R.C. Majumdar correctly observed and I quote: “Bankim Chandra converted patriotism into religion, and religion into patriotism.” These were the criticisms voiced in those days, and I cannot ignore them. We must acknowledge them … HON. CHAIRPERSON (SHRI DILIP SAIKIA): You kindly keep in mind the historic importance and the emotion of the people of our country. You are doing post mortem of Vande Mataram. It should not be like that. Having outlined the elements that fostered resentment in the minds of Muslims, I now turn to the events that unfolded during the period of division in this song. By the 1930s, unrest and communal tensions had reached a peak which was coined as an outcry that compelled the Congress, with its Hindu and Muslim members, to respond. What kind of response? In April 1937, Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru issued a statement on behalf of the Congress and I quote: “It must be remembered that the Congress has always had a large number of Muslims in its fold. Some of our most eminent national leaders have been, and are Muslims. But it is true that the Muslim masses have been largely neglected by us in recent years. We want to correct that omission and carry the message of the Congress to them.” This Press statement was issued by Nehru Ji. Nehru Ji wanted to correct it, but people are accusing that Nehru Ji did it. Was it unilateral by Nehru? We have to go with the documents and credentials available even now with us. October, 1937 is a crucial and historic month in India’s history since the country’s future destination was moulded for a fair democratic discussion notwithstanding the critics who have clouded it. The correction process started in the month of October, 1937. Nehru Ji wrote a letter to Mr. Bose. In that letter, He wrote: “While the present outcry against Vande Mataram was to a large extent a manufactured one by the communalists, there is also some substance to the grievances that needed to be met, and people who were communally inclined have been affected by it. Whatever we do, cannot be to pander to the communalist feeling but to meet the real grievances where they exist.” Nehru Ji wrote to Subhash Chandra Bose having taken cognizance of the matter, and gave the Press a report that they would go according to the credentials as per the facts and history. … It is available there. You get it. … Yes, you are correct. Mr. Bose was in favour of the full song. … I will come to it; do not worry. Mr. Bose was requested by Nehru Ji to write to Tagore and get the details. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel wrote a letter to Tagore asking for details. Dr. Rajendra Prasad wrote a letter to Tagore asking for his opinion. Why was this man Tagore a central person for everybody? A beautiful song was sung by my friend Gogoi and I quote: *(“Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high; Where knowledge is free; Where the world has not been broken up into fragments by narrow domestic walls; Where words come out from the depth of truth; Where tireless striving stretches its arms toward perfection; Where the clear stream of reason has not lost its way into the dreary desert sand of dead habit; Where the mind is led forward by Thee into ever-widening thought and action - Into that heaven of freedom, my Father, let my country awake.”) Why was such trust shown in Tagore? Tagore was the first person in 1896 in Calcutta Session who used to sing Vande Mataram with music. People including Sardar Patel, Dr. Rajendra Prasad, Pandit Nehru and Subhash Chandra Bose pushed the ball to Tagore. HON. CHAIRPERSON (SHRI DILIP SAIKIA): Please conclude now. Sir, give me a few minutes. HON. CHAIRPERSON: Your time is over. Sir, you can adjust some time from the Congress Party also. … Meanwhile, the matter was seized by the Congress Working Committee and it was scheduled for discussion on 26.10.1937. Six days prior to the schedule, Nehru Ji wrote a letter to Subhash Chandra Bose on 20.10.2037. HON. CHAIRPERSON: Your time is over. *()OriginalinTamil Sir, there were a lot of disturbances. Sir, Nehru said and I quote: “I have managed to get an English translation of Anandamath, and I am reading it at present to get the background of the song. It does seem, this background is likely to irritate the Muslims.” This is Nehru.… This is chronological. Sir, I will connect the chain. So, the ball has been shifted to Tagore to decide. Meanwhile, the Congress Working Committee says it will be considered on 26th October. On 20th October, Nehru wrote to Bose. Now, the matter was referred to a sub-committee in the Congress. Who were all the members of the sub-committee? These were Maulana Abul Kalam Azad, Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru, Subhash Chandra Bose, and Narendra Dev. The sub-committee was formed with stipulation to take the advice of the Rabindranath Tagore. So, Tagore became the centre. Now, what did Tagore do? The claim of Tagore on record was: “I was the first person to sing the song Vande Mataram before a gathering of the Calcutta Congress in the year 1896.” That is the reason the entire trust was given to him. Then, Tagore wrote, categorically: “I freely concede that the whole Bankim’s Vande Mataram poem, read together with its context, is liable to be interpreted in ways that might wound Muslims feelings.” To me, again, this spirit of tenderness and devotion expressed in the first portion of the poem: “The emphasis is given to beautiful and beneficial species as motherland. ” Now, Tagore took a decision that this cannot be permitted.… Sir, out of the chain of actions, who is aggrieved? In March, 1937 Jinnah wrote a letter to Gandhi and Nehru that the entire Vande Mataram, not twisted in the alteration that has been done by you, is not acceptable to us, not acceptable to the Muslim League. Sir, my point is, by making a division, the Prime Minister says, you did the appeasement to Muslims. On the contrary, record says, Muslims are aggrieved, then I can say that appeasement was done in favour of Hindus. Sir, all these things are very important. … Sir, then, in April 1939, the entire matter was placed before Mahatma Gandhi, and he took a decision that this poem is not fit to play in the public forum. Finished! Sir, later, on 24th January 1950, it was accepted by the Constituent Assembly. Sir, I am entitled to say this because of the behaviour and the conduct of this Government for the last 11 years, what is the dream of Mahatma, as coined by Prime Minister, I can say. I cannot interpret the dream of Vande Mataram. I am not competent to do that, but I can visualise the dream of Prime Minister. Your dream through the Vande Mataram is to establish a Hindu Rashtra. HON. CHAIRPERSON (SHRI DILIP SAIKIA): Please conclude. Sir, I would like to conclude in three lines. I have come across somewhere recently, a small three-line poem: “Moses split the sea. Prophet Mohammed split the moon. Prime Minister split this country in terms of religion” Thank you. HON. CHAIRPERSON: The word … (Not recorded) must be expunged. 1445 hours
