Speaker: Magunta Sreenivasulu Reddy — Ongole
Speech: Thank you very much, hon. Chairperson, Sir for giving an opportunity for me to speak on 150th anniversary of the National Song: Vande Mataram. Sir, I rise today on behalf of my party and my leader Sri Nara Chandrababu Naidu Garu with great humility and pride to speak on a subject that lies at the heart of our national identity, our freedom struggle, and our national song: Vande Mataram. Sir, this hymn is not merely poetry. It was a call to awaken a sleeping nation to take back control. It was a call towards a religion of patriotism. It placed a motherland, our Bharat Mata, at the center of devotion, sacrifice and duty. Sir, from Bengal to Bombay, from Amritsar to Andhra Pradesh, this song became the marching rhythm of our freedom struggle. Sir, if you know, the popular music director, who has composed music for this song also, like A. R. Rahman ji and Hariharan ji, if you hear the song, you will get Goosebumps.This song provokes everybody to feel happy. Sir, the song Vande Mataram has also gone totally into the minds of the children. It has great impact on their minds. Sir, now, I would like to talk about the role of Andhra Pradesh in the freedom movement. The spirit of Vande Mataram stirred the youth of Andhra Pradesh as well. In 1938, students at Osmania University in Hyderabad challenged the Nizam's ban on singing Vande Mataram. Sir, at that time, the brave 350 students were expelled and also, our former Prime Minister P.V. Narsimha Rao was there. National leaders praised their bravery and condemned the repression of their voice by the Nizam. Being brave in the face of wrong has never been difficult for my people and perhaps one name shines in this regard, a name that fills every heart in Andhra Pradesh with pride, Maanyam Veerudu Alluri Sitaram Raju. His story is not merely a history, it is an epic of courage, sacrifice and love for his people. Alluri Sitarama Raju was a courageous leader, who shook the British Empire. When British soldiers surrounded him and shot at him, he shouted 'Vande Mataram'. Sir, he was the greatest man. When he was surrendered by the British Army, when they were shooting him also, he has shouted “Vande Mataram.” 1900 hours (Kumari Selja in the Chair) Sir, Alluri Sitaram Raju was born in 19th century. By the age of 18, he became a sanyasi and died at the age of 27. He always worked for the development of all tribal communities. His life was short but his impact was eternal. He showed that resistance was not merely an act. For the people of Andhra Pradesh, Alluri is not a chapter in a textbook. He is like a legacy, a living memory, a source of pride that echoes in every hill, every river, every household. His death at the tender age of 27 brought an early end to a brilliant man. Mahatama Gandhi honored his bravery. Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru called him a hero and Netaji Subhash Chandra Bose praised his determination and supreme sacrifice. Sir, mentioning the freedom movement in Andhra Pradesh, I would like to state that from Andhra Kesari Tanguturi Prakasam to Narsimha Reddy to Acharya NG Ranga, we are culminating the glory of Alluri Sitaram Raju. This shows that it is our responsibility to ensure that the courage of our freedom fighters is never forgotten. Their stories must forever live in the minds and hearts of our youth. With these words, I bow my head to the heroes of our land and to Bharat Mata. I love my India. Jai Bharat, Jai Andhra and Vande Mataram. 1901 hours

Speaker: Maddila Gurumoorthy — Tirupati(Sc)
Speech: Thank you, hon. Chairperson Madam, for giving me the opportunity to speak on Vande Mataram. Hon. Chairperson, Madam, Vande Mataram was written by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee about 150 years ago. It is much more than a song. It is a salute to our motherland. Vande Mataram inspires ordinary Indians to become fearless freedom fighters and reminds them that the nation’s honor and the people’s welfare are sacred. Hon. Chairperson, Madam, that spirit demands that every Government and every institute protects the dignity, health and future of its citizens, especially of the poorest and the most vulnerable sections. Recent leaders like Shri YS Jagan Mohan Reddy, with their focus on welfare, social justice and transparent delivery of schemes to mothers, farmers, students, and the poor, represent a modern continuation of that spirit of service to Bharat Mata. Yet in recent times, considering my State of Andhra Pradesh, there have been painful lapses that go against this ideal. …The growing push towards the privatization of medical education is making access to medical colleges and healthcare careers a privilege for a few instead of a service to many people. Farmers are struggling without assured Minimum Support Price and are being pushed into distress, despite feeding the nation…. Last-mile delivery failures, where welfare schemes are announced with great fanfare, do not effectively reach beneficiaries on the ground. There is shocking negligence where school children have been hospitalized after consuming substandard food in hostels and mid-day meals…. There have been deeply disturbing incidents of sexual assault in campuses. Madam, these are not just administrative failures, but they are moral betrayals that wound the soul of the Republic and defeat the very purpose of the great history and spirit of Vande Mataram. Hon. Chairperson, Madam, true patriotism today means not only celebrating our National Song and our glorious past, but also having the courage to confront these injustices; to demand accountability; and to work tirelessly so that every policy, every budget, and every decision reflects genuine respect for Bharat Mata and her children. Vande Mataram. Jai Hind. 1906 hours

Speaker: Tangella Uday Srinivas — Kakinada
Speech: Hon. Chairperson and respected Members of the House, I rise today on behalf of Jana Sena Party with immense pride to speak on the occasion of the 150th anniversary of Vande Mataram, a song that is not just words and melody, but the soul of our nation’s struggle for freedom. Vande Mataram was composed by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee in 1875 and later immortalized in his novel Anandmath. It became a unifying chant for Indians across the country. Every time an Indian sang this song, it inspired courage, unity, and a resolve to reclaim our freedom. Singing it is an act of resistance, a declaration that India would never bow down to the colonial rule. Across the country, the call for freedom inspired countless communities and along the coast of Andhra Pradesh, East Godavari emerged as one of the most vibrant centers of resistance. Before the era of non-violence, East Godavari had already produced fearless warriors who took up arms for freedom. The foremost among them was Dwarabandala Chandraya, the fearless revolutionary who led an armed rebellion against the British in the late 19th century. With unmatched courage, Chandraya Dora united locals and tribals. Communities struck at colonial outposts and challenged the might of the empire at a time when rebellion meant certain death. His sacrifice stands as a proud reminder that the flame of patriotism burned intensely in Andhra Pradesh even before national movements took shape. But East Godavari’s contribution went beyond armed resistance. It also gave India inspiring leaders like Duvvuri Subbamma, who mobilized women to join the nationalist cause fearlessly and stood firm. She faced imprisonment while organizing communities, inspiring countless women to participate in meetings, protests and civil disobedience. The involvement of women from Andhra was a powerful reminder that the struggle for freedom was truly inclusive, drawing courage and commitment from every segment of society. Building on the spirit of mass awakening, East Godavari became a foreground region for Gandhi’s civilian disobedience. While nations watched Gandhi march from Sabarmati to Dandi, the same wave of resistance rose on our shores. The Salt Satyagraha in Uppada and Kothapalli of Pithapuram taluk became East Godavari’s answer to Gandhi. Volunteers walked to the beach, boiled seawater and made salt in open protests against British authority. Even when faced with raids and lathi charges, the people refused to step back, ensuring the Andhra coast stood shoulder to shoulder with Gujarat in the national movement. Today, the spirit of Vande Mataram continues to guide us under the leadership of our hon. Prime Minister Narendra Modi ji. The spirit of national unity and cultural pride has become stronger across the country. His emphasis on Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat reinforces the very ideals that Vande Mataram embodies -- unity, resilience and unwavering love for our motherland. In response to renewed external claims over Arunachal Pradesh, every citizen proudly sang the National Song on the streets, in schools and public spaces. Their courage demonstrates that no pressure can shake their Indian identity or other devotion to the nation. Sir, we celebrate 150 years of Vande Mataram. Let us carry its spirit forward, supporting patriotism, inspiring the next generation, and working together to build a stronger nation, a united and prosperous India. Jai Hind. 1959 hours

Speaker: Kalyan Banerjee — Srerampur
Speech: Thank you hon. Chairperson, Madam, it has been just sung. As we celebrate 150 years of this timeless masterpiece, let us remember, great nations are not built by monuments or markets alone. They are built by the stories they choose to honor, the values ​​they choose to preserve, and the songs they choose to pass on to the next generation. If we want our children to inherit not only a prosperous India but a soulful India, then we must pass down not just the words of Vande Mataram, but the sense of responsibility it awakens. As we stand at this 150 year milestone, let us rise once again not as spectators of history, but as carriers of its flame. Let us say with the same depth, the same modesty, and the same unshaken pride that our freedom fighters once felt – Vande Mataram, I salute thee, Mother. Madam, today's debate was commenced with a political undertone. Today, we gather not for a ritual, not for a formality, but to honor 150 years of a song that once held our entire freedom movement together like a spine. A song that traveled from Bengal to the rest of India not as a poem, but as a pulse – Vande Mataram. When Bankim Chandra wrote those two words in 1875, he could not have known that one day they would rise higher than any speech, any slogan, any political promise. He was writing quietly, almost secretly. No empire could defeat it. No jail could silence it. No bullet could overpower it. Because this song was not created for politics, it was created for the soul. But unfortunately, today, it has been made a political debate purpose. Freedom fighters did not sing Vande Mataram to show power. They sang it to remember who they were. They sang it as they walked to jail, as they faced lathis, as they burned foreign cloth, as they marched barefoot under the burning sun with only – the strength of loving their motherland. Today, 150 years later, we must ask ourselves an honest question: Are we worthy inheritors of that legacy, the resilience of a civilization that had endured a thousand storms and still stood unbent? When the British banned its public recital, the song became even more dangerous and even more beloved. It was whispered in the prison cells by young revolutionaries who did not fear death. It was sung on streets during the Swadeshi movement when common people found uncommon courage. It was carried on the lips of protesters who had nothing in their hands except the trembling but invincible power of hope. Subhash Chandra Bose, Bhagat Singh, Rash Behari Bose, Chittaranjan Das, Aurobindo, leaders of different temperaments, but hearts tuned to the same rhythm – all found their fire stoked by these very words. This was not merely a song, it was a heartbeat shared by the millions. It was a National Song in spirit before it was in status; before India had a Constitution; before India had a Parliament, before India even had a flag of its own. Vande Mataram means unity. It means not uniformity, but unity; not rhetoric, but responsibility; not loudness, but depth. Perhaps, this is why the Song still moves us. It calls upon something timeless, the instinctive feelings of belonging somewhere, of being part of a continuum that stretches backward beyond memory and forward beyond imagination. On this 150th year, let us not only celebrate the past, let us understand it, interpret it, and carry it forward with the intellectual honesty it deserves. Vande Mataram is not just a salute, but a reminder of the Mother whose soil sustains us, whose history humbles us, and whose future depends on us. It blends Sanskritic imagery with the emotional directions of Bengali. It merges geography with spirituality. It elevates the idea of ​​Motherland from a physical territory to a moral force. During the Swadeshi Movement, it transformed into a cultural weapon, one that rejuvenated confidence where people had been taught to feel powerless. It united diverse communities, regions, and social backgrounds under one emotional umbrella. And yet, despite its political use, the Song itself remains above politics; its heart, its culture, civilizational, ethical. Today, in an age of rapid modernization, globalization, and ideological conflict, Vande Mataram offers a reminder of what nationhood truly means, not uniformity. Madam, I will just conclude. Yet, Bankimchandra did not write a national anthem in the modern sense, he wrote a devotional hymn to the lands, to the rivers, to the abundance and the divinity that Indian civilization had always recognized as feminine, as nurturing, as sacred. In that sense, Vande Mataram is not merely a literary composition, it is a civilizational metaphor. This Song is structurally remarkable. Vande Mataram. I salute the Mother. Thank you, Madam. at 2048

Re: Need to upgrade Bhilwara-Ladpura National Highway No. 758 as a four-laning road

Speaker: Anurag Sharma — Jhansi
Speech: Sir, I wish to draw the attention of the Government to the urgent concerns related to the Ken–Betwa Link Project and its impact on the Bundelkhand region, particularly Tehroli Tehsil of Jhansi district. Under the Ken–Betwa Link Project, I request the Hon'ble Minister to clarify the proposed water allocation quotas for Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh, along with the dead storage capacity earmarked for this project. It is a matter of serious concern that 53 non-irrigated villages of Tehroli Tehsil—including Parsa, Raura, Bakayan, Bamnua, Tai, Majra Tai, Rayanara, Gundha, Gatta, Basari, Tehroli Kila, Guresa, Bhagera, Nora, Hanti, Dubkhai, Banka Pahadi, among others—have been awaiting assured irrigation for decades. I urge the Government to clearly state what arrangements have been made to integrate these villages into the command area of ​​the project so that farmers of Tehroli receive their rightful share of water. I further request the Hon'ble Minister to specify whether any definite timeline has been set for completing the irrigation components related specifically to Jhansi district, and particularly Tehroli Tehsil, under the Ken–Betwa Link Project. Re: Need to take comprehensive measures to check illegal immigration of Bangladeshis into Tripura and other North-Eastern States SHRIMATI KRITI DEVI DEBBARMAN (TRIPURA EAST): I wish to draw the attention of the House to the important issue of illegal immigration from Bangladesh into Tripura and other North-Eastern states, which continues to pose challenge to our national security. The Government has taken some commendable steps in recent years to improve border infrastructure, fencing, and surveillance along the Indo-Bangladesh border. However, due to the geographical and topographical complexity of the region, some gaps remain, leading to continued infiltration and unauthorized settlements in certain areas of Tripura. In this context, I request the Hon'ble Minister to kindly provide an update on the implementation of this directive, especially in Tripura, which has historically been a gateway for illegal cross-border movement due to its long and porous international boundary. I also urge the Government to continue its efforts and consider the following: Expedite full border fencing and deploy advanced surveillance systems across vulnerable stretches in Tripura and other affected areas; ensure robust coordination between central and state agencies, including BSF, local police, and administrative authorities, to effectively implement the MHA’s 30-day protocol; and share updated data on detection, detention, and deportation efforts, so that the public is aware of the proactive measures being taken. Re: Need to ensure completion of the doubling of the Konkan Railway route project

Speaker: Kota Srinivas Poojary — Udupi Chikmagalur
Speech: It has been officially placed on record that Karnataka, Maharashtra, and even Goa have requested to exit from Konkan Railway Corporation Limited, and few of them expressed inability to fund the doubling of the route, yet the Corporation continues as an independent PSU instead of being merged with Indian Railways. As a result, the vital West Coast corridor from Mangaluru to Mumbai is still a single-line section, operating at nearly 175% capacity, purely due to fund constraints. KRCL does not have the financial strength to undertake doubling of this high-density route. I wish to know what concrete steps the Government of India and the Ministry of Railways have planned to ensure the long-pending doubling of the Konkan Railway line even if its not merged with Indian Railways. Re: Need to ensure MSP procurement for maize and timely payment by sugar mills to sugarcane growers

Speaker: Darshan Singh Choudhary — Hoshangabad
Speech: Procurement at Minimum Support Price (MSP) is an important agricultural policy of the Government of India that supports farmers and ensures seamless food supply. Farmers are directly benefiting from the increased MSP by the Central Government. In the current situation, intermediaries are taking advantage of the decline in the price of maize. Farmers' maize is being sold in the market for Rs 1000 to Rs 1400 per kg, due to which the cost to the farmer is also coming down by the same amount. Therefore, purchase of maize at Minimum Support Price (MSP) is very important as it will provide economic security to the farmers by giving them a fixed and fair price for their hard work and will help in keeping the farmer income stable. Which will encourage them to grow maize in future also. The government can use the maize purchased at MSP to serve purposes like food supply as well as ethanol production. The additional deduction being made by sugar mills from sugarcane farmers should be changed and a time limit for payment should be fixed. (Evat) Re: Need to accord approval for establishment of Farmers Producer Organization (FPO) in Jhalawar–Baran Parliamentary Constituency SHRI DUSHYANT SINGH (JHALAWAR-BARAN): I wish to draw the attention of the Government to the need for expediting support for agricultural value- chain development in my Parliamentary Constituency Jhalawar–Baran, Rajasthan. While orange in Jhalawar and garlic in Baran have already been designated under the One District One Product (ODOP) framework, farmers continue to face challenges including post-harvest losses, inadequate processing and storage infrastructure, and low-price realisation. Hence, there is an urgent need to operationalize the benefits of ODOP through establishment of additional Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and targeted infrastructure support so that farmers can access branding, packaging, processing, and market linkages under Central schemes. I therefore urge the Government to accelerate approval and handholding support for new FPOs in Jhalawar and Baran, strengthen post-harvest and processing infrastructure and facilitate market linkages and branding for ODOP-designated products. (ends) Re: Need to expedite completion of Murtizapur-Achalpur and Murtizapur- Yavatmal Gauge Conversion (Shakuntala Railway) project in Maharashtra.

Speaker: Gaurav Gogoi — Jorhat
Speech: More than 3,000 small tea growers in Assam are facing acute economic distress due to a steep fall in green tea leaf prices. Rates have dropped to ₹15 per kilogram, while production costs remain close to ₹25 per kilogram, causing daily losses and threatening the long-term viability of small growers. Grower groups report that several Bought Leaf Factories (BLFs) are keeping prices artificially low, citing oversupply and quality issues, while also sourcing raw tea leaves from Arunachal Pradesh and Nagaland, further depressing local price realization and undermining Assam’s producers. There are also widespread reports of non-compliance with the Minimum Benchmark Price set by District Green Leaf Price Monitoring Committees, leading to further erosion of farmer incomes. In response, the All Assam Small Tea Growers’ Association has sought corrective steps, including fixation of a Minimum Support Price, strict enforcement and upward revision of the Minimum Benchmark Price, limits on low-priced tea imports, regulation of inter-state leaf movement, and a scientific grading system to ensure fair price discovery. Given the severity of the crisis and the sector’s importance to rural livelihoods, the Government must act immediately to stabilize prices, enforce regulations, and protect the economic security of Assam’s small tea growers. Re: Need to include Haryana as a co-host for all international events to be held in India.

Speaker: Deepender Singh Hooda — Rohtak
Speech: I wish to raise the matter of Haryana’s exclusion from the hosting plan for the 2030 Commonwealth Games, for which India has recently secured the bid. Haryana contributes nearly half of India’s medals in major international events despite having only around three percent of the country’s population. Players from Haryana have consistently brought national pride through their performance in the Olympics, Asian Games and Commonwealth Games. The people of Haryana wish to participate in this national moment of pride. Cities of Haryana have strong sporting cultures and can benefit significantly from upgraded stadiums, training centers and related infrastructure. Hosting a few events in Haryana would encourage young athletes, expose them to world-class competitions, and strengthen India’s long-term sporting potential. The Government of India must therefore consider including Haryana as a co-host for select events of the 2030 Commonwealth Games so that the State’s contribution to Indian sports is duly reflected. In this context, I also urge that the Union Government support Haryana’s preparedness to bid for hosting rights in future international events such as the 2036 Olympics, whenever such opportunities arise. Re: Need to expedite completion of the Outer Regional Ring Railway line Project in Hyderabad, Telangana

Speaker: Chamala Kiran Kumar Reddy — Bhongir
Speech: I would like to bring to your kind notice regarding dire need to expedite proposed India's First-of-its-kind Outer Regional Ring Railway line project in Hyderabad stretching about 400kms covering about 8 districts and 14 mandals having with about 5 Rail over bridges and also Rail Fly Overs with an estimated project cost of about Rs 12,000 crore encircling Hyderabad running parallel to the upcoming outer Regional Ring Road. Survey portion aligning North side completed and Survey along South side is going on to finalize the DPR also. Considering the importance and development of Hyderabad, Hon'ble Chief Minister of the State of Telangana has taken up this project as a prestigious one to align with Telangana Rising Vision 2047: Your Voice, Your Vision, Our Future & Telangana aims to achieve a $1 trillion economy by 2034 and a $3 trillion economy by 2047 and the state's plan focuses on industrial growth, innovation, social development, and increasing its contribution to India's overall. economy and also for India's Vision for a 55 Trillion Economy and to achieve the theme and goal of Viksit Rajya-Viksit Bharat-2047. I am proud to say that this Ring Rail project will stimulate economic activity, IT hubs, pharmaceutical clusters, logistics parks, and emerging urban growth centers and unlock new growth corridors across the State which would help urbanise 60% of the State to meet future needs to boost Telangana's logistics and mobility ecosystem thereby spurring job creation and industrial growth across the region to become part of Viksit Rajya for Viksit Bharat-2047. Therefore, I request the Hon'ble Minister of Railways, to kindly intervene in the matter to expedite this project. Re: Need to increase procurement quota of paddy and cotton along with need for additional godowns and rakes for their transportation in Telangana.

Speaker: Kadiyam Kavya — Warangal(Sc)
Speech: I request the Hon’ble Minister of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare to state the quantum and value of paddy and cotton procured under Minimum Support Price (MSP) operations in Telangana during the last three most recent Rabi and Kharif marketing seasons, including the number of farmers benefited and the quantity procured through Rythu Vedikas and Primary Agricultural Cooperative Societies (PACS) in the State. In view of an expected bumper harvest, the Government of Telangana has requested that the Kharif procurement target for 2025–26 be increased to 53.6 lakh metric tonnes (LMT) of rice, equivalent to 80 LMT of paddy. The State urgently needs this enhancement along with support for storage and logistics, including hiring additional godowns and allocation of more rakes for transportation to ensure smooth movement. Re: Need to constitute an expert Task Force to study the Air Quality Index and take pollution control measures in Kochi in Kerala

Speaker: Hibi Eden — Ernakulam
Speech: I rise to bring the House’s attention to the alarming environmental crisis unfolding in Kochi, Kerala. The city's Air Quality Index has plummeted to an "Unhealthy" status, with Particulate Matter levels far exceeding safe limits. This situation manifests in thick, smog-like conditions and the formation of dangerous photochemical fog, raising serious fears of Kochi rapidly becoming the next Delhi. The crisis is driven by unchecked pollution from multiple sources. Industrial emissions from belts like Eloor, massive dust generated by ongoing construction projects, and rising vehicular exhaust are choking the air. A critical failure is the lack of foresight in urban planning, specifically the removal of vital tree cover, which has severely hampered the natural dispersion of pollutants. We must treat this as a public health catastrophe. I urge the Union Government to immediately constitute an expert task force to study the localized causes, implement stringent pollution control measures across industrial and construction sectors, and fund urgent reforestation drives. We must act decisively now to safeguard the health of Kochi's citizens and prevent irreversible environmental damage. Re: Need to extend Jabalpur-Hazrat Nizamuddin Sridhaam superfast express Train No. 12191/12192 to Sri Ganganagar

(Evat) Re: Formation of ‘National Anti Fake Drug Task Force’ to combat counterfeit drugs nationwide

Speaker: Thanga Tamil Selvan — Theni
Speech: The ongoing DPR (Detailed Project Report) for construction of bypass on the Usilampatti-Theni-Bodinayakkanur section of the National Highway Section facing Several bottlenecks in this corridor have been repeatedly highlighted by the public, This corridor may be split into multiple sections with each section may be sanctioned as a stand-alone project based on its specific requirements. Secondly the bypasses at Usilampatti, Aundipatti, and Bodinayakkanur (Bodi) is a long-standing public demand due to the narrow and congested town stretches, which lead to frequent delays and safety concerns for road users and the DPR for the entire stretch is currently underway. Construction of Theni Bypass is also equally an essential. Although the Theni stretch has four-lane configuration, the at-grade junction with the State Highway at G Vilaku often results in accidents. The Government Hospital located on the National Highway adds to heavy local traffic. Bypassing the Theni town stretch will help reduce congestion, improve safety, and avoid considerable travel delays experienced by commuters and through-traffic. The Bodi (Bodinayakkanur) Bypass is one of the most consistent demands raised by the public and the available width is extremely narrow even for an intermediate-lane standard, causing chronic congestion and frequent delays. Further, Its alignment be finalized and issued without further delay, considering the high level of daily traffic inconvenience in this stretch.In view of the above, I urge upon the Union Govt. for Priority Approval and Phased Implementation of the above mentioned bypass works in the Usilampatti-Theni-Bodinayakkanur (Bodi) National Highway Section which will considerably reduce travel delays and greatly benefit the people of Theni District and ensure safer, faster, and more reliable connectivity along this vital National Highway corridor. Re: Need to establish a district hospital along with a critical care block under Pradhan Mantri Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission in Konaseema, Andhra Pradesh

Speaker: Ganti Harish Madhur — Amalapuram(Sc)
Speech: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar Konaseema District faces critical gaps in essential healthcare infrastructure, exposing its rural and disaster- prone population to serious risks. The absence of a District Hospital has left the district without any secondary healthcare facilities, compelling patients to depend on neighboring districts during medical emergencies. The district is also devoid of trauma and poly-trauma centers and a Critical Care Block. This structural vacuum severely undermines the timely management of accident and critical cases. Several Primary Health Centers remain in a state of disrepair. Furthermore, only nine Community Health Centers cater to a population exceeding 17 lakh, highlighting a serious mismatch between population size and available infrastructure. Maternal and neonatal healthcare services remain overburdened due to the absence of Special Newborn Care Units. Although a Day Care Cancer Center has been approved, construction is yet to commence, leaving cancer patients without local access to essential oncology care. In view of these deficiencies, it is imperative that the Ministry take expeditious measures to establish a District Hospital in Konaseema, sanction a Critical Care Block under PM ABHIM, operationalise the sanctioned Day Care Cancer Centre, and bridge the remaining infrastructural gaps. Re: Need to formulate a rehabilitation policy in coordination with State Government to reclaim the encroached railway land in Maharashtra

Speaker: Shrikant Shinde — Kalyan
Speech: The work of railway station redevelopment and modernization is being severely hampered nationwide due to encroachment of railway land. A striking example is the situation in states like Maharashtra, where millions of families have been residing in unauthorized structures on railway land for years, and are now receiving eviction notices, as mandated by orders from the Hon'ble Supreme Court. This problem is not limited to station upgrades. Crucial railway expansion projects, including the 5th and 6th lines, have also been put in cold storage owing to encroachment on railway land. The railways are unable to reclaim their land, leading to significant delays in essential infrastructure projects. Given that these poor families have been residing in these locations for a long time and often lack valid documentation regarding their housing, forcibly evicting such a large number of people is not only an immense socio-economic challenge but also a human rights issue. Therefore, I request the Central Government in consultation with the State Government of Maharashtra to formulate a plan, on the lines of slum rehabilitation policy, to find a permanent solution to this humanitarian and developmental challenge. This joint policy must be framed to allow the Railways to reclaim its land and resolve the hindrance to station redevelopment work caused by encroachment. Re:NeedtobringBithalpurairportunderUDANscheme

Speaker: Misa Bharti — Pataliputra
Speech: Jamui district holds immense importance from religious and historical point of view. Here is Lachuad, the birthplace of Lord Mahavir, the 24th Tirthankar of Jainism, where lakhs of devotees come from all over the world including Maharashtra, North Prussia, Rajasthan and Delhi. Due to lack of airway facilities, these devotees have to face extreme difficulties in transportation. The feasibility of Bathalpur airport has been proved by the landing of the government aircraft of the then Chief Minister in 1995. This air base can be easily developed to operate small aircraft with a capacity of up to 19 seats. Its launch will not only provide convenience to Jain devotees but will also open new doors of employment and development for the residents of Jamui as well as neighboring districts like Banka, Magura, Shekhpura, Lakhisarai and Nada. Therefore, I strongly urge the Civil Aviation Minister to immediately approve the inclusion of Jamui's Bithalpur airport in the flight plan and the Airports Authority of India (AAI) should be invested to start its construction work soon. This topic is related to the realization of the Prime Minister's 'Ude Sha' (common citizen) slogan. (Re:NeedtobringaNationalpolicytoensureuniformserviceconditionsforASHAand otherhealthworkersinthecountry)

Speaker: K. Subbarayan — Tiruppur
Speech: I rise to draw the urgent attention of this House to the long-standing grievances of ASHA workers-the Accredited Social Health Activists-who form the backbone of India's primary healthcare system. Grant ASHA workers permanent status. Fix a minimum monthly wage of not less than ₹26,000. Ensure timely payment of wages and incentives through a transparent, accountable mechanism. Extend all social security benefits, including EPF, ESI, maternity leave, paid leave, pension, and health insurance. Provide periodic training, safety equipment, and travel allowances given the nature of their fieldwork. Frame a national policy ensuring uniform service conditions for ASHA and frontline health workers across states. Re:IllegalsandmininginSonRiverinBihar

Speaker: Sudama Prasad — Arrah
Speech: Unabated illegal sand mining has become a serious problem in Bihar. This mining, especially on the banks of Son River, is an ecological disaster. The villages and important plants and trees along the banks are disappearing. The workers earning the required daily wages on this mining site, where they currently get only ₹ 200, are making a just demand to increase their minimum wage to ₹ 500. But instead of accepting these routes, the crore earning contractor has arrested about 22 protesting workers. We demand from the Central and State Governments that all the 22 arrested workers should be released immediately and unconditionally. All illegal sand mining taking place in Son and other rivers should be immediately stopped. Wherever legal mining is taking place, a minimum standard wage of ₹500 should be immediately implemented for workers. The Government should ensure widespread migration to alternative jobs with better wages and permanent employment for these mine workers as well as all those affected by the sand digging operations. A permanent moratorium on all evictions carried out without fulfilling the statutory obligation of ‘first migration’ should be introduced. (Evat) Re: Need for development of old NH-744 from Chinnakkada to Edamon in Kerala

Speaker: N. K. Premachandran — Kollam
Speech: The old NH 744 from Chinnakkada to Edamon requires comprehensive development. The existing road is not sufficient to meet the heavy traffic requirement. The construction of new Greenfield Highway is not sufficient for the heavy traffic requirement. The development of old National Highway is inevitable because it passes through many important junctions. So many Government institutions, educational institutions, hospitals, religious institutions are dependent on this route. The area is densely populated. The construction of new Greenfield Highway is better for long journey. The demand for developing old highway 744 from Chinnakkada to Edamon is pending with the authorities. Once the authorities considered the same and a project was approved. Subsequently the project was dropped. The reason for dropping the development activities is not known. Hence I urge upon the Government to initiate immediate action for developing old NH 744 from Chinnakkada to Edamon. Re: Enhancing SDRF support land eligibility to 5 hectares to get relief for disaster affected farmers in Rajasthan

Speaker: Hanuman Beniwal — Nagaur
Speech: I would like to draw the attention of the government towards increasing the agricultural input subsidy for horticulture and cash crops and increasing the maximum limit of agricultural input subsidy from 2 hectares to 5 hectares and tell that the initial cost of horticulture crops like Satara, Kannu and cash crops like cumin, isabgol etc. is very high but Under the SCRF norms, in case of failure in these categories of crops, agricultural input subsidy of only Rs 22,500 per hectare has been given, which should be increased to a minimum of Rs 1 lakh per hectare.
